     194              : /*
     195              :  * Are we doing recovery from XLOG?
     196              :  *
     197              :  * This is only ever true in the startup process; it should be read as meaning
     198              :  * "this process is replaying WAL records", rather than "the system is in
     199              :  * recovery mode".  It should be examined primarily by functions that need
     200              :  * to act differently when called from a WAL redo function (e.g., to skip WAL
     201              :  * logging).  To check whether the system is in recovery regardless of which
     202              :  * process you're running in, use RecoveryInProgress() but only after shared
     203              :  * memory startup and lock initialization.
     204              :  */
     205              : bool            InRecovery = false;

     256              :  *              -1: must check RecoveryInProgress(); disallow until it is false
     257              :  * Most processes start with -1 and transition to 1 after seeing that recovery
     258              :  * is not in progress.  But we can also force the value for special cases.
     259              :  * The coding in XLogInsertAllowed() depends on the first two of these states
     260              :  * being numerically the same as bool true and false.
     261              :  */
     262              : static int      LocalXLogInsertAllowed = -1;

     268              :  * valid in the startup process.
     269              :  *
     270              :  * When ArchiveRecoveryRequested is true, but InArchiveRecovery is false, we're
     271              :  * currently performing crash recovery using only XLOG files in pg_wal, but
     272              :  * will switch to using offline XLOG archives as soon as we reach the end of
     273              :  * WAL in pg_wal.
     274              : */

     324              : 
     325              : /*
     326              :  * During normal operation, the only timeline we care about is ThisTimeLineID.
     327              :  * During recovery, however, things are more complicated.  To simplify life
     328              :  * for rmgr code, we keep ThisTimeLineID set to the "current" timeline as we
     329              :  * scan through the WAL history (that is, it is the line that was active when
     330              :  * the currently-scanned WAL record was generated).  We also need these
     331              :  * timeline values:
     332              :  *
     333              :  * recoveryTargetTimeLineGoal: what the user requested, if any
     334              :  *
     335              :  * recoveryTargetTLIRequested: numeric value of requested timeline, if constant
     336              :  *
     337              :  * recoveryTargetTLI: the currently understood target timeline; changes
     338              :  *
     339              :  * expectedTLEs: a list of TimeLineHistoryEntries for recoveryTargetTLI and the timelines of
     340              :  * its known parents, newest first (so recoveryTargetTLI is always the
     341              :  * first list member).  Only these TLIs are expected to be seen in the WAL
     342              :  * segments we read, and indeed only these TLIs will be considered as
     343              :  * candidate WAL files to open at all.
     344              :  *
     345              :  * curFileTLI: the TLI appearing in the name of the current input WAL file.
     346              :  * (This is not necessarily the same as ThisTimeLineID, because we could
     347              :  * be scanning data that was copied from an ancestor timeline when the current
     348              :  * file was created.)  During a sequential scan we do not allow this value
     349              :  * to decrease.
     350              :  */
     351              : RecoveryTargetTimeLineGoal recoveryTargetTimeLineGoal = RECOVERY_TARGET_TIMELINE_LATEST;
     352              : TimeLineID      recoveryTargetTLIRequested = 0;
     353              : TimeLineID      recoveryTargetTLI = 0;
     354              : static List *expectedTLEs;
     355              : static TimeLineID curFileTLI;
     356              : 
     357              : /*
     358              :  * ProcLastRecPtr points to the start of the last XLOG record inserted by the
     359              :  * current backend.  It is updated for all inserts.  XactLastRecEnd points to
     360              :  * end+1 of the last record, and is reset when we end a top-level transaction,
     361              :  * or start a new one; so it can be used to tell if the current transaction has
     362              :  * created any XLOG records.
     363              :  *
     364              :  * While in parallel mode, this may not be fully up to date.  When committing,
     365              :  * a transaction can assume this covers all xlog records written either by the
     366              :  * user backend or by any parallel worker which was present at any point during
     367              :  * the transaction.  But when aborting, or when still in parallel mode, other
     368              :  * parallel backends may have written WAL records at later LSNs than the value
     369              :  * stored here.  The parallel leader advances its own copy, when necessary,
     370              :  * in WaitForParallelWorkersToFinish.
     371              :  */
     372              : XLogRecPtr      ProcLastRecPtr = InvalidXLogRecPtr;
     373              : XLogRecPtr      XactLastRecEnd = InvalidXLogRecPtr;
     374              : XLogRecPtr      XactLastCommitEnd = InvalidXLogRecPtr;
     375              : 
     376              : /*
     377              :  * RedoRecPtr is this backend's local copy of the REDO record pointer
     378              :  * (which is almost but not quite the same as a pointer to the most recent
     379              :  * CHECKPOINT record).  We update this from the shared-memory copy,
     380              :  * XLogCtl->Insert.RedoRecPtr, whenever we can safely do so (ie, when we
     381              :  * hold an insertion lock).  See XLogInsertRecord for details.  We are also
     382              :  * allowed to update from XLogCtl->RedoRecPtr if we hold the info_lck;
     383              :  * see GetRedoRecPtr.  A freshly spawned backend obtains the value during
     384              :  * InitXLOGAccess.
     385              :  */
     386              : static XLogRecPtr RedoRecPtr;
     387              : 
     388              : /*
     389              :  * doPageWrites is this backend's local copy of (forcePageWrites ||
     390              :  * fullPageWrites).  It is used together with RedoRecPtr to decide whether
     391              :  * a full-page image of a page need to be taken.
     392              :  */
     393              : static bool doPageWrites;
     394              : 
     395              : /* Has the recovery code requested a walreceiver wakeup? */
     396              : static bool doRequestWalReceiverReply;
     397              : 
     398              : /*
     399              :  * RedoStartLSN points to the checkpoint's REDO location which is specified
     400              :  * in a backup label file, backup history file or control file. In standby
     401              :  * mode, XLOG streaming usually starts from the position where an invalid
     402              :  * record was found. But if we fail to read even the initial checkpoint
     403              :  * record, we use the REDO location instead of the checkpoint location as
     404              :  * the start position of XLOG streaming. Otherwise we would have to jump
     405              :  * backwards to the REDO location after reading the checkpoint record,
     406              :  * because the REDO record can precede the checkpoint record.
     407              :  */
     408              : static XLogRecPtr RedoStartLSN = InvalidXLogRecPtr;
     409              : 
     410              : /*----------
     411              :  * Shared-memory data structures for XLOG control
     412              :  *
     413              :  * LogwrtRqst indicates a byte position that we need to write and/or fsync
     414              :  * the log up to (all records before that point must be written or fsynced).
     415              :  * LogwrtResult indicates the byte positions we have already written/fsynced.
     416              :  * These structs are identical but are declared separately to indicate their
     417              :  * slightly different functions.
     418              :  *
     419              :  * To read XLogCtl->LogwrtResult, you must hold either info_lck or
     420              :  * WALWriteLock.  To update it, you need to hold both locks.  The point of
     421              :  * this arrangement is that the value can be examined by code that already
     422              :  * holds WALWriteLock without needing to grab info_lck as well.  In addition
     423              :  * to the shared variable, each backend has a private copy of LogwrtResult,
     424              :  * which is updated when convenient.
     425              :  *
     426              :  * The request bookkeeping is simpler: there is a shared XLogCtl->LogwrtRqst
     427              :  * (protected by info_lck), but we don't need to cache any copies of it.
     428              :  *
     429              :  * info_lck is only held long enough to read/update the protected variables,
     430              :  * so it's a plain spinlock.  The other locks are held longer (potentially
     431              :  * over I/O operations), so we use LWLocks for them.  These locks are:
     432              :  *
     433              :  * WALBufMappingLock: must be held to replace a page in the WAL buffer cache.
     434              :  * It is only held while initializing and changing the mapping.  If the
     435              :  * contents of the buffer being replaced haven't been written yet, the mapping
     436              :  * lock is released while the write is done, and reacquired afterwards.
     437              :  *
     438              :  * WALWriteLock: must be held to write WAL buffers to disk (XLogWrite or
     439              :  * XLogFlush).
     440              :  *
     441              :  * ControlFileLock: must be held to read/update control file or create
     442              :  * new log file.
     443              :  *
     444              :  * CheckpointLock: must be held to do a checkpoint or restartpoint (ensures
     445              :  * only one checkpointer at a time; currently, with all checkpoints done by
     446              :  * the checkpointer, this is just pro forma).
     447              :  *
     448              :  *----------
     449              :  */
     450              : 
     451              : typedef struct XLogwrtRqst
     452              : {
     453              :         XLogRecPtr      Write;                  /* last byte + 1 to write out */
     454              :         XLogRecPtr      Flush;                  /* last byte + 1 to flush */
     455              : } XLogwrtRqst;
     456              : 
     457              : typedef struct XLogwrtResult
     458              : {
     459              :         XLogRecPtr      Write;                  /* last byte + 1 written out */
     460              :         XLogRecPtr      Flush;                  /* last byte + 1 flushed */
     461              : } XLogwrtResult;
     462              : 
     463              : /*
     464              :  * Inserting to WAL is protected by a small fixed number of WAL insertion
     465              :  * locks. To insert to the WAL, you must hold one of the locks - it doesn't
     466              :  * matter which one. To lock out other concurrent insertions, you must hold
     467              :  * of them. Each WAL insertion lock consists of a lightweight lock, plus an
     468              :  * indicator of how far the insertion has progressed (insertingAt).
     469              :  *
     470              :  * The insertingAt values are read when a process wants to flush WAL from
     471              :  * the in-memory buffers to disk, to check that all the insertions to the
     472              :  * region the process is about to write out have finished. You could simply
     473              :  * wait for all currently in-progress insertions to finish, but the
     474              :  * insertingAt indicator allows you to ignore insertions to later in the WAL,
     475              :  * so that you only wait for the insertions that are modifying the buffers
     476              :  * you're about to write out.
     477              :  *
     478              :  * This isn't just an optimization. If all the WAL buffers are dirty, an
     479              :  * inserter that's holding a WAL insert lock might need to evict an old WAL
     480              :  * buffer, which requires flushing the WAL. If it's possible for an inserter
     481              :  * to block on another inserter unnecessarily, deadlock can arise when two
     482              :  * inserters holding a WAL insert lock wait for each other to finish their
     483              :  * insertion.
     484              :  *
     485              :  * Small WAL records that don't cross a page boundary never update the value,
     486              :  * the WAL record is just copied to the page and the lock is released. But
     487              :  * to avoid the deadlock-scenario explained above, the indicator is always
     488              :  * updated before sleeping while holding an insertion lock.
     489              :  *
     490              :  * lastImportantAt contains the LSN of the last important WAL record inserted
     491              :  * using a given lock. This value is used to detect if there has been
     492              :  * important WAL activity since the last time some action, like a checkpoint,
     493              :  * was performed - allowing to not repeat the action if not. The LSN is
     494              :  * updated for all insertions, unless the XLOG_MARK_UNIMPORTANT flag was
     495              :  * set. lastImportantAt is never cleared, only overwritten by the LSN of newer
     496              :  * records.  Tracking the WAL activity directly in WALInsertLock has the
     497              :  * advantage of not needing any additional locks to update the value.
     498              :  */
     499              : typedef struct
     500              : {
     501              :         LWLock          lock;
     502              :         XLogRecPtr      insertingAt;
     503              :         XLogRecPtr      lastImportantAt;
     504              : } WALInsertLock;
     505              : 
     506              : /*
     507              :  * All the WAL insertion locks are allocated as an array in shared memory. We
     508              :  * force the array stride to be a power of 2, which saves a few cycles in
     509              :  * indexing, but more importantly also ensures that individual slots don't
     510              :  * cross cache line boundaries. (Of course, we have to also ensure that the
     511              :  * array start address is suitably aligned.)
     512              :  */
     513              : typedef union WALInsertLockPadded
     514              : {
     515              :         WALInsertLock l;
     516              :         char            pad[PG_CACHE_LINE_SIZE];
     517              : } WALInsertLockPadded;
     518              : 
     519              : /*
     520              :  * State of an exclusive backup, necessary to control concurrent activities
     521              :  * across sessions when working on exclusive backups.
     522              :  *
     523              :  * EXCLUSIVE_BACKUP_NONE means that there is no exclusive backup actually
     524              :  * running, to be more precise pg_start_backup() is not being executed for
     525              :  * an exclusive backup and there is no exclusive backup in progress.
     526              :  * EXCLUSIVE_BACKUP_STARTING means that pg_start_backup() is starting an
     527              :  * exclusive backup.
     528              :  * EXCLUSIVE_BACKUP_IN_PROGRESS means that pg_start_backup() has finished
     529              :  * running and an exclusive backup is in progress. pg_stop_backup() is
     530              :  * needed to finish it.
     531              :  * EXCLUSIVE_BACKUP_STOPPING means that pg_stop_backup() is stopping an
     532              :  * exclusive backup.
     533              :  */
     534              : typedef enum ExclusiveBackupState
     535              : {
     536              :         EXCLUSIVE_BACKUP_NONE = 0,
     537              :         EXCLUSIVE_BACKUP_STARTING,
     538              :         EXCLUSIVE_BACKUP_IN_PROGRESS,
     539              :         EXCLUSIVE_BACKUP_STOPPING
     540              : } ExclusiveBackupState;
     541              : 
     542              : /*
     543              :  * Session status of running backup, used for sanity checks in SQL-callable
     544              :  * functions to start and stop backups.
     545              :  */
     546              : static SessionBackupState sessionBackupState = SESSION_BACKUP_NONE;
     547              : 
     548              : /*
     549              :  * Shared state data for WAL insertion.
     550              :  */
     551              : typedef struct XLogCtlInsert
     552              : {
     553              :         slock_t         insertpos_lck;  /* protects CurrBytePos and PrevBytePos */
     554              : 
     555              :         /*
     556              :          * CurrBytePos is the end of reserved WAL. The next record will be
     557              :          * inserted at that position. PrevBytePos is the start position of the
     558              :          * previously inserted (or rather, reserved) record - it is copied to the
     559              :          * prev-link of the next record. These are stored as "usable byte
     560              :          * positions" rather than XLogRecPtrs (see XLogBytePosToRecPtr()).
     561              :          */
     562              :         uint64          CurrBytePos;
     563              :         uint64          PrevBytePos;
     564              : 
     565              :         /*
     566              :          * Make sure the above heavily-contended spinlock and byte positions are
     567              :          * on their own cache line. In particular, the RedoRecPtr and full page
     568              :          * write variables below should be on a different cache line. They are
     569              :          * read on every WAL insertion, but updated rarely, and we don't want
     570              :          * those reads to steal the cache line containing Curr/PrevBytePos.
     571              :          */
     572              :         char            pad[PG_CACHE_LINE_SIZE];
     573              : 
     574              :         /*
     575              :          * fullPageWrites is the master copy used by all backends to determine
     576              :          * whether to write full-page to WAL, instead of using process-local one.
     577              :          * This is required because, when full_page_writes is changed by SIGHUP,
     578              :          * we must WAL-log it before it actually affects WAL-logging by backends.
     579              :          * Checkpointer sets at startup or after SIGHUP.
     580              :          *
     581              :          * To read these fields, you must hold an insertion lock. To modify them,
     582              :          * you must hold ALL the locks.
     583              :          */
     584              :         XLogRecPtr      RedoRecPtr;             /* current redo point for insertions */
     585              :         bool            forcePageWrites;        /* forcing full-page writes for PITR? */
     586              :         bool            fullPageWrites;
     587              : 
     588              :         /*
     589              :          * exclusiveBackupState indicates the state of an exclusive backup (see
     590              :          * comments of ExclusiveBackupState for more details). nonExclusiveBackups
     591              :          * is a counter indicating the number of streaming base backups currently
     592              :          * in progress. forcePageWrites is set to true when either of these is
     593              :          * non-zero. lastBackupStart is the latest checkpoint redo location used
     594              :          * as a starting point for an online backup.
     595              :          */
     596              :         ExclusiveBackupState exclusiveBackupState;
     597              :         int                     nonExclusiveBackups;
     598              :         XLogRecPtr      lastBackupStart;
     599              : 
     600              :         /*
     601              :          * WAL insertion locks.
     602              :          */
     603              :         WALInsertLockPadded *WALInsertLocks;
     604              : } XLogCtlInsert;
     605              : 
     606              : /*
     607              :  * Total shared-memory state for XLOG.
     608              :  */
     609              : typedef struct XLogCtlData
     610              : {
     611              :         XLogCtlInsert Insert;
     612              : 
     613              :         /* Protected by info_lck: */
     614              :         XLogwrtRqst LogwrtRqst;
     615              :         XLogRecPtr      RedoRecPtr;             /* a recent copy of Insert->RedoRecPtr */
     616              :         FullTransactionId ckptFullXid;  /* nextFullXid of latest checkpoint */
     617              :         XLogRecPtr      asyncXactLSN;   /* LSN of newest async commit/abort */
     618              :         XLogRecPtr      replicationSlotMinLSN;  /* oldest LSN needed by any slot */
     619              : 
     620              :         XLogSegNo       lastRemovedSegNo;       /* latest removed/recycled XLOG segment */
     621              : 
     622              :         /* Fake LSN counter, for unlogged relations. Protected by ulsn_lck. */
     623              :         XLogRecPtr      unloggedLSN;
     624              :         slock_t         ulsn_lck;
     625              : 
     626              :         /* Time and LSN of last xlog segment switch. Protected by WALWriteLock. */
     627              :         pg_time_t       lastSegSwitchTime;
     628              :         XLogRecPtr      lastSegSwitchLSN;
     629              : 
     630              :         /*
     631              :          * Protected by info_lck and WALWriteLock (you must hold either lock to
     632              :          * read it, but both to update)
     633              :          */
     634              :         XLogwrtResult LogwrtResult;
     635              : 
     636              :         /*
     637              :          * Latest initialized page in the cache (last byte position + 1).
     638              :          *
     639              :          * To change the identity of a buffer (and InitializedUpTo), you need to
     640              :          * hold WALBufMappingLock.  To change the identity of a buffer that's
     641              :          * still dirty, the old page needs to be written out first, and for that
     642              :          * you need WALWriteLock, and you need to ensure that there are no
     643              :          * in-progress insertions to the page by calling
     644              :          * WaitXLogInsertionsToFinish().
     645              :          */
     646              :         XLogRecPtr      InitializedUpTo;
     647              : 
     648              :         /*
     649              :          * These values do not change after startup, although the pointed-to pages
     650              :          * and xlblocks values certainly do.  xlblocks values are protected by
     651              :          * WALBufMappingLock.
     652              :          */
     653              :         char       *pages;                      /* buffers for unwritten XLOG pages */
     654              :         XLogRecPtr *xlblocks;           /* 1st byte ptr-s + XLOG_BLCKSZ */
     655              :         int                     XLogCacheBlck;  /* highest allocated xlog buffer index */
     656              : 
     657              :         /*
     658              :          * Shared copy of ThisTimeLineID. Does not change after end-of-recovery.
     659              :          * If we created a new timeline when the system was started up,
     660              :          * PrevTimeLineID is the old timeline's ID that we forked off from.
     661              :          * Otherwise it's equal to ThisTimeLineID.
     662              :          */
     663              :         TimeLineID      ThisTimeLineID;
     664              :         TimeLineID      PrevTimeLineID;
     665              : 
     666              :         /*
     667              :          * SharedRecoveryState indicates if we're still in crash or archive
     668              :          * recovery.  Protected by info_lck.
     669              :          */
     670              :         RecoveryState SharedRecoveryState;
     671              : 
     672              :         /*
     673              :          * SharedHotStandbyActive indicates if we allow hot standby queries to be
     674              :          * run.  Protected by info_lck.
     675              :          */
     676              :         bool            SharedHotStandbyActive;
     677              : 
     678              :         /*
     679              :          * InstallXLogFileSegmentActive indicates whether the checkpointer should
     680              :          * arrange for future segments by recycling and/or PreallocXlogFiles().
     681              :          * Protected by ControlFileLock.  Only the startup process changes it.  If
     682              :          * true, anyone can use InstallXLogFileSegment().  If false, the startup
     683              :          * process owns the exclusive right to install segments, by reading from
     684              :          * the archive and possibly replacing existing files.
     685              :          */
     686              :         bool            InstallXLogFileSegmentActive;
     687              : 
     688              :         /*
     689              :          * SharedPromoteIsTriggered indicates if a standby promotion has been
     690              :          * triggered.  Protected by info_lck.
     691              :          */
     692              :         bool            SharedPromoteIsTriggered;
     693              : 
     694              :         /*
     695              :          * WalWriterSleeping indicates whether the WAL writer is currently in
     696              :          * low-power mode (and hence should be nudged if an async commit occurs).
     697              :          * Protected by info_lck.
     698              :          */
     699              :         bool            WalWriterSleeping;
     700              : 
     701              :         /*
     702              :          * recoveryWakeupLatch is used to wake up the startup process to continue
     703              :          * WAL replay, if it is waiting for WAL to arrive or failover trigger file
     704              :          * to appear.
     705              :          */
     706              :         Latch           recoveryWakeupLatch;
     707              : 
     708              :         /*
     709              :          * During recovery, we keep a copy of the latest checkpoint record here.
     710              :          * lastCheckPointRecPtr points to start of checkpoint record and
     711              :          * lastCheckPointEndPtr points to end+1 of checkpoint record.  Used by the
     712              :          * checkpointer when it wants to create a restartpoint.
     713              :          *
     714              :          * Protected by info_lck.
     715              :          */
     716              :         XLogRecPtr      lastCheckPointRecPtr;
     717              :         XLogRecPtr      lastCheckPointEndPtr;
     718              :         CheckPoint      lastCheckPoint;
     719              : 
     720              :         /*
     721              :          * lastReplayedEndRecPtr points to end+1 of the last record successfully
     722              :          * replayed. When we're currently replaying a record, ie. in a redo
     723              :          * function, replayEndRecPtr points to the end+1 of the record being
     724              :          * replayed, otherwise it's equal to lastReplayedEndRecPtr.
     725              :          */
     726              :         XLogRecPtr      lastReplayedEndRecPtr;
     727              :         TimeLineID      lastReplayedTLI;
     728              :         XLogRecPtr      replayEndRecPtr;
     729              :         TimeLineID      replayEndTLI;
     730              :         /* timestamp of last COMMIT/ABORT record replayed (or being replayed) */
     731              :         TimestampTz recoveryLastXTime;
     732              : 
     733              :         /*
     734              :          * timestamp of when we started replaying the current chunk of WAL data,
     735              :          * only relevant for replication or archive recovery
     736              :          */
     737              :         TimestampTz currentChunkStartTime;
     738              :         /* Are we requested to pause recovery? */
     739              :         bool            recoveryPause;
     740              : 
     741              :         /*
     742              :          * lastFpwDisableRecPtr points to the start of the last replayed
     743              :          * XLOG_FPW_CHANGE record that instructs full_page_writes is disabled.
     744              :          */
     745              :         XLogRecPtr      lastFpwDisableRecPtr;
     746              : 
     747              :         slock_t         info_lck;               /* locks shared variables shown above */
     748              : } XLogCtlData;
     749              : 
     750              : static XLogCtlData *XLogCtl = NULL;
     751              : 
     752              : /* a private copy of XLogCtl->Insert.WALInsertLocks, for convenience */
     753              : static WALInsertLockPadded *WALInsertLocks = NULL;
     754              : 
     755              : /*
     756              :  * We maintain an image of pg_control in shared memory.
     757              :  */
     758              : static ControlFileData *ControlFile = NULL;
     759              : 
     760              : /*
     761              :  * Calculate the amount of space left on the page after 'endptr'. Beware
     762              :  * multiple evaluation!
     763              :  */
     764              : #define INSERT_FREESPACE(endptr)        \
     765              :         (((endptr) % XLOG_BLCKSZ == 0) ? 0 : (XLOG_BLCKSZ - (endptr) % XLOG_BLCKSZ))
     766              : 
     767              : /* Macro to advance to next buffer index. */
     768              : #define NextBufIdx(idx)         \
     769              :                 (((idx) == XLogCtl->XLogCacheBlck) ? 0 : ((idx) + 1))
     770              : 
     771              : /*
     772              :  * XLogRecPtrToBufIdx returns the index of the WAL buffer that holds, or
     773              :  * would hold if it was in cache, the page containing 'recptr'.
     774              :  */
     775              : #define XLogRecPtrToBufIdx(recptr)      \
     776              :         (((recptr) / XLOG_BLCKSZ) % (XLogCtl->XLogCacheBlck + 1))
     777              : 
     778              : /*
     779              :  * These are the number of bytes in a WAL page usable for WAL data.
     780              :  */
     781              : #define UsableBytesInPage (XLOG_BLCKSZ - SizeOfXLogShortPHD)
     782              : 
     783              : /*
     784              :  * Convert values of GUCs measured in megabytes to equiv. segment count.
     785              :  * Rounds down.
     786              :  */
     787              : #define ConvertToXSegs(x, segsize)      XLogMBVarToSegs((x), (segsize))
     788              : 
     789              : /* The number of bytes in a WAL segment usable for WAL data. */
     790              : static int      UsableBytesInSegment;
     791              : 
     792              : /*
     793              :  * Private, possibly out-of-date copy of shared LogwrtResult.
     794              :  * See discussion above.
     795              :  */
     796              : static XLogwrtResult LogwrtResult = {0, 0};
     797              : 
     798              : /*
     799              :  * Codes indicating where we got a WAL file from during recovery, or where
     800              :  * to attempt to get one.
     801              :  */
     802              : typedef enum
     803              : {
     804              :         XLOG_FROM_ANY = 0,                      /* request to read WAL from any source */
     805              :         XLOG_FROM_ARCHIVE,                      /* restored using restore_command */
     806              :         XLOG_FROM_PG_WAL,                       /* existing file in pg_wal */
     807              :         XLOG_FROM_STREAM                        /* streamed from master */
     808              : } XLogSource;
     809              : 
     810              : /* human-readable names for XLogSources, for debugging output */
     811              : static const char *const xlogSourceNames[] = {"any", "archive", "pg_wal", "stream"};
     812              : 
     813              : /*
     814              :  * openLogFile is -1 or a kernel FD for an open log file segment.
     815              :  * openLogSegNo identifies the segment.  These variables are only used to
     816              :  * write the XLOG, and so will normally refer to the active segment.
     817              :  * Note: call Reserve/ReleaseExternalFD to track consumption of this FD.
     818              :  */
     819              : static int      openLogFile = -1;
     820              : static XLogSegNo openLogSegNo = 0;
     821              : 
     822              : /*
     823              :  * These variables are used similarly to the ones above, but for reading
     824              :  * the XLOG.  readOff is the offset of the page just read, readLen
     825              :  * indicates how much of it has been read into readBuf, and readSource
     826              :  * indicates where we got the currently open file from.
     827              :  * Note: we could use Reserve/ReleaseExternalFD to track consumption of
     828              :  * this FD too; but it doesn't currently seem worthwhile, since the XLOG is
     829              :  * not read by general-purpose sessions.
     830              :  */
     831              : static int      readFile = -1;
     832              : static XLogSegNo readSegNo = 0;
     833              : static uint32 readOff = 0;
     834              : static uint32 readLen = 0;
     835              : static XLogSource readSource = XLOG_FROM_ANY;
     836              : 
     837              : /*
     838              :  * Keeps track of which source we're currently reading from. This is
     839              :  * different from readSource in that this is always set, even when we don't
     840              :  * currently have a WAL file open. If lastSourceFailed is set, our last
     841              :  * attempt to read from currentSource failed, and we should try another source
     842              :  * next.
     843              :  *
     844              :  * pendingWalRcvRestart is set when a config change occurs that requires a
     845              :  * walreceiver restart.  This is only valid in XLOG_FROM_STREAM state.
     846              :  */
     847              : static XLogSource currentSource = XLOG_FROM_ANY;
     848              : static bool lastSourceFailed = false;
     849              : static bool pendingWalRcvRestart = false;
     850              : 
     851              : typedef struct XLogPageReadPrivate
     852              : {
     853              :         int                     emode;
     854              :         bool            fetching_ckpt;  /* are we fetching a checkpoint record? */
     855              :         bool            randAccess;
     856              : } XLogPageReadPrivate;
     857              : 
     858              : /*
     859              :  * These variables track when we last obtained some WAL data to process,
     860              :  * and where we got it from.  (XLogReceiptSource is initially the same as
     861              :  * readSource, but readSource gets reset to zero when we don't have data
     862              :  * to process right now.  It is also different from currentSource, which
     863              :  * also changes when we try to read from a source and fail, while
     864              :  * XLogReceiptSource tracks where we last successfully read some WAL.)
     865              :  */
     866              : static TimestampTz XLogReceiptTime = 0;
     867              : static XLogSource XLogReceiptSource = XLOG_FROM_ANY;
     868              : 
     869              : /* State information for XLOG reading */
     870              : static XLogRecPtr ReadRecPtr;   /* start of last record read */
     871              : static XLogRecPtr EndRecPtr;    /* end+1 of last record read */
     872              : 
     873              : /*
     874              :  * Local copies of equivalent fields in the control file.  When running
     875              :  * crash recovery, minRecoveryPoint is set to InvalidXLogRecPtr as we
     876              :  * expect to replay all the WAL available, and updateMinRecoveryPoint is
     877              :  * switched to false to prevent any updates while replaying records.
     878              :  * Those values are kept consistent as long as crash recovery runs.
     879              :  */
     880              : static XLogRecPtr minRecoveryPoint;
     881              : static TimeLineID minRecoveryPointTLI;
     882              : static bool updateMinRecoveryPoint = true;
     883              : 
     884              : /*
     885              :  * Have we reached a consistent database state? In crash recovery, we have
     886              :  * to replay all the WAL, so reachedConsistency is never set. During archive
     887              :  * recovery, the database is consistent once minRecoveryPoint is reached.
     888              :  */
     889              : bool            reachedConsistency = false;
     890              : 
     891              : static bool InRedo = false;
     892              : 
     893              : /* Have we launched bgwriter during recovery? */
     894              : static bool bgwriterLaunched = false;
     895              : 
     896              : /* For WALInsertLockAcquire/Release functions */
     897              : static int      MyLockNo = 0;
     898              : static bool holdingAllLocks = false;
     899              : 
     900              : #ifdef WAL_DEBUG
     901              : static MemoryContext walDebugCxt = NULL;
     902              : #endif

     996              : /*
     997              :  * Insert an XLOG record represented by an already-constructed chain of data
     998              :  * chunks.  This is a low-level routine; to construct the WAL record header
     999              :  * and data, use the higher-level routines in xloginsert.c.
    1000              :  *
    1001              :  * If 'fpw_lsn' is valid, it is the oldest LSN among the pages that this
    1002              :  * WAL record applies to, that were not included in the record as full page
    1003              :  * images.  If fpw_lsn <= RedoRecPtr, the function does not perform the
    1004              :  * insertion and returns InvalidXLogRecPtr.  The caller can then recalculate
    1005              :  * which pages need a full-page image, and retry.  If fpw_lsn is invalid, the
    1006              :  * record is always inserted.
    1007              :  *
    1008              :  * 'flags' gives more in-depth control on the record being inserted. See
    1009              :  * XLogSetRecordFlags() for details.
    1010              :  *
    1011              :  * The first XLogRecData in the chain must be for the record header, and its
    1012              :  * data must be MAXALIGNed.  XLogInsertRecord fills in the xl_prev and
    1013              :  * xl_crc fields in the header, the rest of the header must already be filled
    1014              :  * by the caller.
    1015              :  *
    1016              :  * Returns XLOG pointer to end of record (beginning of next record).
    1017              :  * This can be used as LSN for data pages affected by the logged action.
    1018              :  * (LSN is the XLOG point up to which the XLOG must be flushed to disk
    1019              :  * before the data page can be written out.  This implements the basic
    1020              :  * WAL rule "write the log before the data".)
    1021              :  */

    1039              :         /* we assume that all of the record header is in the first chunk */

    1042              :         /* cross-check on whether we should be here or not */

    1045              : 
    1046              :         /*----------
    1047              :          *
    1048              :          * We have now done all the preparatory work we can without holding a
    1049              :          * lock or modifying shared state. From here on, inserting the new WAL
    1050              :          * record to the shared WAL buffer cache is a two-step process:
    1051              :          *
    1052              :          * 1. Reserve the right amount of space from the WAL. The current head of
    1053              :          *        reserved space is kept in Insert->CurrBytePos, and is protected by
    1054              :          *        insertpos_lck.
    1055              :          *
    1056              :          * 2. Copy the record to the reserved WAL space. This involves finding the
    1057              :          *        correct WAL buffer containing the reserved space, and copying the
    1058              :          *        record in place. This can be done concurrently in multiple processes.
    1059              :          *
    1060              :          * To keep track of which insertions are still in-progress, each concurrent
    1061              :          * inserter acquires an insertion lock. In addition to just indicating that
    1062              :          * an insertion is in progress, the lock tells others how far the inserter
    1063              :          * has progressed. There is a small fixed number of insertion locks,
    1064              :          * determined by NUM_XLOGINSERT_LOCKS. When an inserter crosses a page
    1065              :          * boundary, it updates the value stored in the lock to the how far it has
    1066              :          * inserted, to allow the previous buffer to be flushed.
    1067              :          *
    1068              :          * Holding onto an insertion lock also protects RedoRecPtr and
    1069              :          * fullPageWrites from changing until the insertion is finished.
    1070              :          *
    1071              :          * Step 2 can usually be done completely in parallel. If the required WAL
    1072              :          * page is not initialized yet, you have to grab WALBufMappingLock to
    1073              :          * initialize it, but the WAL writer tries to do that ahead of insertions
    1074              :          * to avoid that from happening in the critical path.
    1075              :          *
    1076              :          *----------
    1077              :          */

    1079        70686 :         if (isLogSwitch)

    1084              :         /*

    1144              :                 /*

    1151              :                 /*

    1295              : /*
    1296              :  * Reserves the right amount of space for a record of given size from the WAL.
    1297              :  * *StartPos is set to the beginning of the reserved section, *EndPos to
    1298              :  * its end+1. *PrevPtr is set to the beginning of the previous record; it is
    1299              :  * used to set the xl_prev of this record.
    1300              :  *
    1301              :  * This is the performance critical part of XLogInsert that must be serialized
    1302              :  * across backends. The rest can happen mostly in parallel. Try to keep this
    1303              :  * section as short as possible, insertpos_lck can be heavily contended on a
    1304              :  * busy system.
    1305              :  *
    1306              :  * NB: The space calculation here must match the code in CopyXLogRecordToWAL,
    1307              :  * where we actually copy the record to the reserved space.
    1308              :  */
    1309              : static void
    1310        70681 : ReserveXLogInsertLocation(int size, XLogRecPtr *StartPos, XLogRecPtr *EndPos,
    1311              :                                                   XLogRecPtr *PrevPtr)
    1312              : {
    1313        70681 :         XLogCtlInsert *Insert = &XLogCtl->Insert;
    1314              :         uint64          startbytepos;
    1315              :         uint64          endbytepos;
    1316              :         uint64          prevbytepos;
    1317              : 
    1318        70681 :         size = MAXALIGN(size);
    1319              : 
    1320              :         /* All (non xlog-switch) records should contain data. */
    1321              :         Assert(size > SizeOfXLogRecord);
    1322              : 
    1323              :         /*
    1324              :          * The duration the spinlock needs to be held is minimized by minimizing
    1325              :          * the calculations that have to be done while holding the lock. The
    1326              :          * current tip of reserved WAL is kept in CurrBytePos, as a byte position
    1327              :          * that only counts "usable" bytes in WAL, that is, it excludes all WAL
    1328              :          * page headers. The mapping between "usable" byte positions and physical
    1329              :          * positions (XLogRecPtrs) can be done outside the locked region, and
    1330              :          * because the usable byte position doesn't include any headers, reserving
    1331              :          * X bytes from WAL is almost as simple as "CurrBytePos += X".
    1332              :          */
    1333        70681 :         SpinLockAcquire(&Insert->insertpos_lck);
    1334              : 
    1335        70681 :         startbytepos = Insert->CurrBytePos;
    1336        70681 :         endbytepos = startbytepos + size;
    1337        70681 :         prevbytepos = Insert->PrevBytePos;
    1338        70681 :         Insert->CurrBytePos = endbytepos;
    1339        70681 :         Insert->PrevBytePos = startbytepos;
    1340              : 
    1341        70681 :         SpinLockRelease(&Insert->insertpos_lck);
    1342              : 
    1343        70681 :         *StartPos = XLogBytePosToRecPtr(startbytepos);
    1344        70681 :         *EndPos = XLogBytePosToEndRecPtr(endbytepos);
    1345        70681 :         *PrevPtr = XLogBytePosToRecPtr(prevbytepos);
    1346              : 
    1347              :         /*
    1348              :          * Check that the conversions between "usable byte positions" and
    1349              :          * XLogRecPtrs work consistently in both directions.
    1350              :          */
    1351              :         Assert(XLogRecPtrToBytePos(*StartPos) == startbytepos);
    1352              :         Assert(XLogRecPtrToBytePos(*EndPos) == endbytepos);
    1353              :         Assert(XLogRecPtrToBytePos(*PrevPtr) == prevbytepos);
    1354        70681 : }
    1355              : 
    1356              : /*

    1358              :  *
    1359              :  * A log-switch record is handled slightly differently. The rest of the
    1360              :  * segment will be reserved for this insertion, as indicated by the returned
    1361              :  * *EndPos value. However, if we are already at the beginning of the current
    1362              :  * segment, *StartPos and *EndPos are set to the current location without
    1363              :  * reserving any space, and the function returns false.
    1364              : */

    1366            0 : ReserveXLogSwitch(XLogRecPtr *StartPos, XLogRecPtr *EndPos, XLogRecPtr *PrevPtr)
    1367              : {
    1368            0 :         XLogCtlInsert *Insert = &XLogCtl->Insert;
    1369              :         uint64          startbytepos;

    1371              :         uint64          prevbytepos;
    1372            0 :         uint32          size = MAXALIGN(SizeOfXLogRecord);

    1376              :         /*

    1386            0 :         ptr = XLogBytePosToEndRecPtr(startbytepos);

    1393              : 
    1394            0 :         endbytepos = startbytepos + size;

    1407            0 :         Insert->CurrBytePos = endbytepos;

    1419            0 :         return true;

    1421              : 
    1422              : /*
    1423              :  * Checks whether the current buffer page and backup page stored in the
    1424              :  * WAL record are consistent or not. Before comparing the two pages, a
    1425              :  * masking can be applied to the pages to ignore certain areas like hint bits,
    1426              :  * unused space between pd_lower and pd_upper among other things. This
    1427              :  * function should be called once WAL replay has been completed for a
    1428              :  * given record.
    1429              :  */
    1430              : static void
    1431            0 : checkXLogConsistency(XLogReaderState *record)
    1432              : {
    1433            0 :         RmgrId          rmid = XLogRecGetRmid(record);

    1436              :         BlockNumber blkno;
    1437              :         int                     block_id;
    1438              : 
    1439              :         /* Records with no backup blocks have no need for consistency checks. */
    1440            0 :         if (!XLogRecHasAnyBlockRefs(record))
    1441            0 :                 return;
    1442              : 
    1443              :         Assert((XLogRecGetInfo(record) & XLR_CHECK_CONSISTENCY) != 0);
    1444              : 
    1445            0 :         for (block_id = 0; block_id <= record->max_block_id; block_id++)
    1446              :         {
    1447              :                 Buffer          buf;
    1448              :                 Page            page;
    1449              : 
    1450            0 :                 if (!XLogRecGetBlockTag(record, block_id, &rnode, &forknum, &blkno))
    1451              :                 {
    1452              :                         /*
    1453              :                          * WAL record doesn't contain a block reference with the given id.
    1454              :                          * Do nothing.
    1455              :                          */

    1458              :

    1675              : /*
    1676              :  * Acquire a WAL insertion lock, for inserting to WAL.
    1677              :  */
    1678              : static void
    1679        70686 : WALInsertLockAcquire(void)
    1680              : {
    1681              :         bool            immed;
    1682              : 
    1683              :         /*
    1684              :          * It doesn't matter which of the WAL insertion locks we acquire, so try
    1685              :          * the one we used last time.  If the system isn't particularly busy, it's
    1686              :          * a good bet that it's still available, and it's good to have some
    1687              :          * affinity to a particular lock so that you don't unnecessarily bounce
    1688              :          * cache lines between processes when there's no contention.
    1689              :          *
    1690              :          * If this is the first time through in this backend, pick a lock
    1691              :          * (semi-)randomly.  This allows the locks to be used evenly if you have a
    1692              :          * lot of very short connections.
    1693              :          */
    1694              :         static int      lockToTry = -1;
    1695              : 
    1696        70686 :         if (lockToTry == -1)
    1697           11 :                 lockToTry = MyProc->pgprocno % NUM_XLOGINSERT_LOCKS;
    1698        70686 :         MyLockNo = lockToTry;
    1699              : 
    1700              :         /*
    1701              :          * The insertingAt value is initially set to 0, as we don't know our

    1704        70686 :         immed = LWLockAcquire(&WALInsertLocks[MyLockNo].l.lock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);

    1707              :                 /*

    1709              :                  * time.  On a system with more insertion locks than concurrent
    1710              :                  * inserters, this causes all the inserters to eventually migrate to a
    1711              :                  * lock that no-one else is using.  On a system with more inserters
    1712              :                  * than locks, it still helps to distribute the inserters evenly
    1713              :                  * across the locks.
    1714              :                  */
    1715            0 :                 lockToTry = (lockToTry + 1) % NUM_XLOGINSERT_LOCKS;
    1716              :         }
    1717        70686 : }

    1719              : /*
    1720              :  * Acquire all WAL insertion locks, to prevent other backends from inserting

    1722              :  */
    1723              : static void
    1724           22 : WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive(void)
    1725              : {
    1726              :         int                     i;
    1727              : 
    1728              :         /*

    1743           22 :         holdingAllLocks = true;

    1745              :

    1797              : /*
    1798              :  * Wait for any WAL insertions < upto to finish.
    1799              :  *
    1800              :  * Returns the location of the oldest insertion that is still in-progress.
    1801              :  * Any WAL prior to that point has been fully copied into WAL buffers, and
    1802              :  * can be flushed out to disk. Because this waits for any insertions older
    1803              :  * than 'upto' to finish, the return value is always >= 'upto'.
    1804              :  *

    1806              :  * *before* acquiring WALWriteLock, to avoid deadlocks. This function might
    1807              :  * need to wait for an insertion to finish (or at least advance to next
    1808              :  * uninitialized page), and the inserter might need to evict an old WAL buffer

    1810              :  */

    1812         1217 : WaitXLogInsertionsToFinish(XLogRecPtr upto)
    1813              : {
    1814              :         uint64          bytepos;
    1815              :         XLogRecPtr      reservedUpto;
    1816              :         XLogRecPtr      finishedUpto;
    1817         1217 :         XLogCtlInsert *Insert = &XLogCtl->Insert;
    1818              :         int                     i;
    1819              : 
    1820         1217 :         if (MyProc == NULL)
    1821            0 :                 elog(PANIC, "cannot wait without a PGPROC structure");
    1822              : 
    1823              :         /* Read the current insert position */
    1824         1217 :         SpinLockAcquire(&Insert->insertpos_lck);
    1825         1217 :         bytepos = Insert->CurrBytePos;
    1826         1217 :         SpinLockRelease(&Insert->insertpos_lck);
    1827         1217 :         reservedUpto = XLogBytePosToEndRecPtr(bytepos);
    1828              : 
    1829              :         /*
    1830              :          * No-one should request to flush a piece of WAL that hasn't even been
    1831              :          * reserved yet. However, it can happen if there is a block with a bogus
    1832              :          * LSN on disk, for example. XLogFlush checks for that situation and
    1833              :          * complains, but only after the flush. Here we just assume that to mean

    1835              :          * corner-case, the return value can be smaller than 'upto' argument.
    1836              :          */
    1837         1217 :         if (upto > reservedUpto)
    1838              :         {

    1840              :                          (uint32) (upto >> 32), (uint32) upto,
    1841              :                          (uint32) (reservedUpto >> 32), (uint32) reservedUpto);
    1842            0 :                 upto = reservedUpto;
    1843              :         }
    1844              : 
    1845              :         /*
    1846              :          * Loop through all the locks, sleeping on any in-progress insert older

    1848              :          *

    1850              :          * the WAL insertions have been finished. Initialize it to the head of
    1851              :          * reserved WAL, and as we iterate through the insertion locks, back it

    1859              :                 do
    1860              :                 {
    1861              :                         /*

    1863              :                          * wait for the lock to be released, or for the 'value' to be set
    1864              :                          * by a LWLockUpdateVar call.  When a lock is initially acquired,
    1865              :                          * its value is 0 (InvalidXLogRecPtr), which means that we don't
    1866              :                          * know where it's inserting yet.  We will have to wait for it. If
    1867              :                          * it's a small insertion, the record will most likely fit on the

    1893              : 
    1894              : /*
    1895              :  * Get a pointer to the right location in the WAL buffer containing the
    1896              :  * given XLogRecPtr.
    1897              :  *
    1898              :  * If the page is not initialized yet, it is initialized. That might require
    1899              :  * evicting an old dirty buffer from the buffer cache, which means I/O.
    1900              :  *
    1901              :  * The caller must ensure that the page containing the requested location
    1902              :  * isn't evicted yet, and won't be evicted. The way to ensure that is to
    1903              :  * hold onto a WAL insertion lock with the insertingAt position set to
    1904              :  * something <= ptr. GetXLogBuffer() will update insertingAt if it needs
    1905              :  * to evict an old page from the buffer. (This means that once you call
    1906              :  * GetXLogBuffer() with a given 'ptr', you must not access anything before
    1907              :  * that point anymore, and must not call GetXLogBuffer() with an older 'ptr'
    1908              :  * later, because older buffers might be recycled already)
    1909              :  */
    1910              : static char *
    1911        71899 : GetXLogBuffer(XLogRecPtr ptr)
    1912              : {
    1913              :         int                     idx;
    1914              :         XLogRecPtr      endptr;
    1915              :         static uint64 cachedPage = 0;
    1916              :         static char *cachedPos = NULL;
    1917              :         XLogRecPtr      expectedEndPtr;
    1918              : 
    1919              :         /*
    1920              :          * Fast path for the common case that we need to access again the same
    1921              :          * page as last time.
    1922              :          */
    1923        71899 :         if (ptr / XLOG_BLCKSZ == cachedPage)
    1924              :         {
    1925              :                 Assert(((XLogPageHeader) cachedPos)->xlp_magic == XLOG_PAGE_MAGIC);
    1926              :                 Assert(((XLogPageHeader) cachedPos)->xlp_pageaddr == ptr - (ptr % XLOG_BLCKSZ));
    1927        70597 :                 return cachedPos + ptr % XLOG_BLCKSZ;
    1928              :         }
    1929              : 
    1930              :         /*
    1931              :          * The XLog buffer cache is organized so that a page is always loaded to a
    1932              :          * particular buffer.  That way we can easily calculate the buffer a given
    1933              :          * page must be loaded into, from the XLogRecPtr alone.
    1934              :          */
    1935         1302 :         idx = XLogRecPtrToBufIdx(ptr);
    1936              : 
    1937              :         /*
    1938              :          * See what page is loaded in the buffer at the moment. It could be the
    1939              :          * page we're looking for, or something older. It can't be anything newer
    1940              :          * - that would imply the page we're looking for has already been written
    1941              :          * out to disk and evicted, and the caller is responsible for making sure
    1942              :          * that doesn't happen.
    1943              :          *
    1944              :          * However, we don't hold a lock while we read the value. If someone has
    1945              :          * just initialized the page, it's possible that we get a "torn read" of
    1946              :          * the XLogRecPtr if 64-bit fetches are not atomic on this platform. In
    1947              :          * that case we will see a bogus value. That's ok, we'll grab the mapping
    1948              :          * lock (in AdvanceXLInsertBuffer) and retry if we see anything else than
    1949              :          * the page we're looking for. But it means that when we do this unlocked
    1950              :          * read, we might see a value that appears to be ahead of the page we're
    1951              :          * looking for. Don't PANIC on that, until we've verified the value while
    1952              :          * holding the lock.
    1953              :          */
    1954         1302 :         expectedEndPtr = ptr;
    1955         1302 :         expectedEndPtr += XLOG_BLCKSZ - ptr % XLOG_BLCKSZ;
    1956              : 
    1957         1302 :         endptr = XLogCtl->xlblocks[idx];
    1958         1302 :         if (expectedEndPtr != endptr)
    1959              :         {
    1960              :                 XLogRecPtr      initializedUpto;
    1961              : 
    1962              :                 /*
    1963              :                  * Before calling AdvanceXLInsertBuffer(), which can block, let others
    1964              :                  * know how far we're finished with inserting the record.
    1965              :                  *
    1966              :                  * NB: If 'ptr' points to just after the page header, advertise a
    1967              :                  * position at the beginning of the page rather than 'ptr' itself. If
    1968              :                  * there are no other insertions running, someone might try to flush
    1969              :                  * up to our advertised location. If we advertised a position after
    1970              :                  * the page header, someone might try to flush the page header, even
    1971              :                  * though page might actually not be initialized yet. As the first
    1972              :                  * inserter on the page, we are effectively responsible for making

    1974              :                  * the page header.
    1975              :                  */

    1993              :         }
    1994              :         else

    2016              : /*

    2222            0 :                                         XLogWrite(WriteRqst, false);

    2225              :                                 }

    2260              :                 /*

    2325              : /*
    2326              :  * Calculate CheckPointSegments based on max_wal_size_mb and
    2327              :  * checkpoint_completion_target.

    2329              : static void
    2330           20 : CalculateCheckpointSegments(void)
    2331              : {
    2332              :         double          target;
    2333              : 
    2334              :         /*-------
    2335              :          * Calculate the distance at which to trigger a checkpoint, to avoid
    2336              :          * exceeding max_wal_size_mb. This is based on two assumptions:
    2337              :          *
    2338              :          * a) we keep WAL for only one checkpoint cycle (prior to PG11 we kept
    2339              :          *    WAL for two checkpoint cycles to allow us to recover from the
    2340              :          *    secondary checkpoint if the first checkpoint failed, though we
    2341              :          *    only did this on the master anyway, not on standby. Keeping just
    2342              :          *    one checkpoint simplifies processing and reduces disk space in
    2343              :          *    many smaller databases.)
    2344              :          * b) during checkpoint, we consume checkpoint_completion_target *
    2345              :          *        number of segments consumed between checkpoints.
    2346              :          *-------
    2347              :          */
    2348           20 :         target = (double) ConvertToXSegs(max_wal_size_mb, wal_segment_size) /
    2349           20 :                 (1.0 + CheckPointCompletionTarget);
    2350              : 
    2351              :         /* round down */
    2352           20 :         CheckPointSegments = (int) target;
    2353              : 
    2354           20 :         if (CheckPointSegments < 1)
    2355            0 :                 CheckPointSegments = 1;
    2356           20 : }
    2357              : 
    2358              : void
    2359           10 : assign_max_wal_size(int newval, void *extra)
    2360              : {

    2362           10 :         CalculateCheckpointSegments();

    2365              : void
    2366            6 : assign_checkpoint_completion_target(double newval, void *extra)

    2371              : 
    2372              : /*
    2373              :  * At a checkpoint, how many WAL segments to recycle as preallocated future
    2374              :  * XLOG segments? Returns the highest segment that should be preallocated.
    2375              :  */
    2376              : static XLogSegNo
    2377            0 : XLOGfileslop(XLogRecPtr lastredoptr)
    2378              : {
    2379              :         XLogSegNo       minSegNo;
    2380              :         XLogSegNo       maxSegNo;
    2381              :         double          distance;
    2382              :         XLogSegNo       recycleSegNo;
    2383              : 
    2384              :         /*
    2385              :          * Calculate the segment numbers that min_wal_size_mb and max_wal_size_mb
    2386              :          * correspond to. Always recycle enough segments to meet the minimum, and
    2387              :          * remove enough segments to stay below the maximum.
    2388              :          */
    2389            0 :         minSegNo = lastredoptr / wal_segment_size +
    2390            0 :                 ConvertToXSegs(min_wal_size_mb, wal_segment_size) - 1;
    2391            0 :         maxSegNo = lastredoptr / wal_segment_size +
    2392            0 :                 ConvertToXSegs(max_wal_size_mb, wal_segment_size) - 1;
    2393              : 
    2394              :         /*
    2395              :          * Between those limits, recycle enough segments to get us through to the
    2396              :          * estimated end of next checkpoint.
    2397              :          *
    2398              :          * To estimate where the next checkpoint will finish, assume that the
    2399              :          * system runs steadily consuming CheckPointDistanceEstimate bytes between
    2400              :          * every checkpoint.
    2401              :          */
    2402            0 :         distance = (1.0 + CheckPointCompletionTarget) * CheckPointDistanceEstimate;
    2403              :         /* add 10% for good measure. */
    2404            0 :         distance *= 1.10;
    2405              : 
    2406            0 :         recycleSegNo = (XLogSegNo) ceil(((double) lastredoptr + distance) /
    2407              :                                                                         wal_segment_size);
    2408              : 
    2409            0 :         if (recycleSegNo < minSegNo)
    2410            0 :                 recycleSegNo = minSegNo;
    2411            0 :         if (recycleSegNo > maxSegNo)
    2412            0 :                 recycleSegNo = maxSegNo;
    2413              : 
    2414            0 :         return recycleSegNo;
    2415              : }
    2416              : 
    2417              : /*
    2418              :  * Check whether we've consumed enough xlog space that a checkpoint is needed.
    2419              :  *
    2420              :  * new_segno indicates a log file that has just been filled up (or read
    2421              :  * during recovery). We measure the distance from RedoRecPtr to new_segno
    2422              :  * and see if that exceeds CheckPointSegments.
    2423              :  *
    2424              :  * Note: it is caller's responsibility that RedoRecPtr is up-to-date.
    2425              :  */
    2426              : static bool
    2427            0 : XLogCheckpointNeeded(XLogSegNo new_segno)
    2428              : {
    2429              :         XLogSegNo       old_segno;
    2430              : 
    2431            0 :         XLByteToSeg(RedoRecPtr, old_segno, wal_segment_size);
    2432              : 
    2433            0 :         if (new_segno >= old_segno + (uint64) (CheckPointSegments - 1))
    2434            0 :                 return true;

    2816              :

    2874              : /*
    2875              :  * Ensure that all XLOG data through the given position is flushed to disk.

    2881         2775 : XLogFlush(XLogRecPtr record)

    2889              :          * test XLogInsertAllowed(), not InRecovery, because we need checkpointer

    2891              :          * end-of-recovery checkpoint, it should indeed flush.

    2894              :         {
    2895            0 :                 UpdateMinRecoveryPoint(record, false);
    2896         1566 :                 return;

    2898              : 
    2899              :         /* Quick exit if already known flushed */

    2901         1566 :                 return;
    2902              : 
    2903              : #ifdef WAL_DEBUG
    2904              :         if (XLOG_DEBUG)
    2905              :                 elog(LOG, "xlog flush request %X/%X; write %X/%X; flush %X/%X",
    2906              :                          (uint32) (record >> 32), (uint32) record,
    2907              :                          (uint32) (LogwrtResult.Write >> 32), (uint32) LogwrtResult.Write,

    2910              : 
    2911         1209 :         START_CRIT_SECTION();
    2912              : 
    2913              :         /*
    2914              :          * Since fsync is usually a horribly expensive operation, we try to
    2915              :          * piggyback as much data as we can on each fsync: if we see any more data
    2916              :          * entered into the xlog buffer, we'll write and fsync that too, so that
    2917              :          * the final value of LogwrtResult.Flush is as large as possible. This
    2918              :          * gives us some chance of avoiding another fsync immediately after.
    2919              :          */

    3026              :          * unable to restart the database at all!  (This scenario actually

    3045              : /*
    3046              :  * Write & flush xlog, but without specifying exactly where to.
    3047              :  *
    3048              :  * We normally write only completed blocks; but if there is nothing to do on
    3049              :  * that basis, we check for unwritten async commits in the current incomplete
    3050              :  * block, and write through the latest one of those.  Thus, if async commits
    3051              :  * are not being used, we will write complete blocks only.
    3052              :  *
    3053              :  * If, based on the above, there's anything to write we do so immediately. But
    3054              :  * to avoid calling fsync, fdatasync et. al. at a rate that'd impact
    3055              :  * concurrent IO, we only flush WAL every wal_writer_delay ms, or if there's
    3056              :  * more than wal_writer_flush_after unflushed blocks.
    3057              :  *
    3058              :  * We can guarantee that async commits reach disk after at most three
    3059              :  * wal_writer_delay cycles. (When flushing complete blocks, we allow XLogWrite
    3060              :  * to write "flexibly", meaning it can stop at the end of the buffer ring;
    3061              :  * this makes a difference only with very high load or long wal_writer_delay,
    3062              :  * but imposes one extra cycle for the worst case for async commits.)

    3065              :  *

    3603              : /*
    3604              :  * Install a new XLOG segment file as a current or future log segment.
    3605              :  *
    3606              :  * This is used both to install a newly-created segment (which has a temp

    3612              :  *
    3613              :  * tmppath: initial name of file to install.  It will be renamed into place.
    3614              :  *
    3615              :  * find_free: if true, install the new segment at the first empty segno
    3616              :  * number at or after the passed numbers.  If false, install the new segment
    3617              :  * exactly where specified, deleting any existing segment file there.
    3618              :  *
    3619              :  * max_segno: maximum segment number to install the new file as.  Fail if no
    3620              :  * free slot is found between *segno and max_segno. (Ignored when find_free
    3621              :  * is false.)
    3622              :  *
    3623              :  * Returns true if the file was installed successfully.  false indicates that
    3624              :  * max_segno limit was exceeded, the startup process has disabled this
    3625              :  * function for now, or an error occurred while renaming the file into place.
    3626              :  */

    3635              : 
    3636            2 :         LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
    3637            2 :         if (!XLogCtl->InstallXLogFileSegmentActive)
    3638              :         {
    3639            0 :                 LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
    3640            0 :                 return false;
    3641              :         }
    3642              : 
    3643            2 :         if (!find_free)
    3644              :         {
    3645              :                 /* Force installation: get rid of any pre-existing segment file */
    3646            1 :                 durable_unlink(path, DEBUG1);
    3647              :         }
    3648              :         else
    3649              :         {
    3650              :                 /* Find a free slot to put it in */

    3652              :                 {
    3653            0 :                         if ((*segno) >= max_segno)
    3654              :                         {
    3655              :                                 /* Failed to find a free slot within specified range */

    3657            0 :                                 return false;
    3658              :                         }

    3660            0 :                         XLogFilePath(path, ThisTimeLineID, *segno, wal_segment_size);

    3667            0 :                 LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
    3668              :                 /* durable_rename already emitted log message */
    3669            0 :                 return false;
    3670              :         }
    3671              : 
    3672            2 :         LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
    3673              : 
    3674            2 :         return true;

    3782              : /*

    3787              : static int
    3788            5 : XLogFileReadAnyTLI(XLogSegNo segno, int emode, XLogSource source)

    3794              : 
    3795              :         /*
    3796              :          * Loop looking for a suitable timeline ID: we might need to read any of

    3798              :          *
    3799              :          * We expect curFileTLI on entry to be the TLI of the preceding file in
    3800              :          * sequence, or 0 if there was no predecessor.  We do not allow curFileTLI
    3801              :          * to go backwards; this prevents us from picking up the wrong file when a
    3802              :          * parent timeline extends to higher segment numbers than the child we
    3803              :          * want to read.
    3804              :          *
    3805              :          * If we haven't read the timeline history file yet, read it now, so that
    3806              :          * we know which TLIs to scan.  We don't save the list in expectedTLEs,
    3807              :          * however, unless we actually find a valid segment.  That way if there is
    3808              :          * neither a timeline history file nor a WAL segment in the archive, and
    3809              :          * streaming replication is set up, we'll read the timeline history file
    3810              :          * streamed from the master when we start streaming, instead of recovering
