    3822              : 
    3823            5 :                 if (tli < curFileTLI)
    3824            0 :                         break;                          /* don't bother looking at too-old TLIs */
    3825              :

    3834            0 :                         XLByteToSeg(hent->begin, beginseg, wal_segment_size);
    3835              : 
    3836              :                         /*

    3839              :                          * ended at, respectively. It's sufficient to check only the
    3840              :                          * starting segment of the timeline here. Since the timelines are
    3841              :                          * scanned in descending order in this loop, any segments newer
    3842              :                          * than the ending segment should belong to newer timeline and
    3843              :                          * have already been read before. So it's not necessary to check

    3885              : /*
    3886              :  * Close the current logfile segment for writing.

    3929              :  *

    3931              :  * trouble; for example, a full filesystem is one cause.  The checkpoint WAL
    3932              :  * and/or ControlFile updates already completed.  If a RequestCheckpoint()
    3933              :  * initiated the present checkpoint and an ERROR ends this function, the
    3934              :  * command that called RequestCheckpoint() fails.  That's not ideal, but it's
    3935              :  * not worth contorting more functions to use caller-specified elevel values.

    4368              : /*
    4369              :  * Attempt to read the next XLOG record.

    4373              :  *

    4375              :  * (emode must be either PANIC, LOG). In standby mode, retries until a valid
    4376              :  * record is available.
    4377              :  */
    4378              : static XLogRecord *
    4379            9 : ReadRecord(XLogReaderState *xlogreader, int emode,
    4380              :                    bool fetching_ckpt)
    4381              : {

    4384              : 
    4385              :         /* Pass through parameters to XLogPageRead */
    4386            9 :         private->fetching_ckpt = fetching_ckpt;
    4387            9 :         private->emode = emode;
    4388            9 :         private->randAccess = (xlogreader->ReadRecPtr == InvalidXLogRecPtr);
    4389              : 
    4390              :         /* This is the first attempt to read this page. */
    4391            9 :         lastSourceFailed = false;
    4392              : 
    4393              :         for (;;)
    4394            0 :         {
    4395              :                 char       *errormsg;
    4396              : 
    4397            9 :                 record = XLogReadRecord(xlogreader, &errormsg);
    4398            9 :                 ReadRecPtr = xlogreader->ReadRecPtr;

    4400            9 :                 if (record == NULL)
    4401              :                 {
    4402              :                         /*
    4403              :                          * When we find that WAL ends in an incomplete record, keep track

    4405              :                          * indicate to downstream WAL readers that that portion is to be
    4406              :                          * ignored.
    4407              :                          *
    4408              :                          * However, when ArchiveRecoveryRequested = true, we're going to
    4409              :                          * switch to a new timeline at the end of recovery. We will only
    4410              :                          * copy WAL over to the new timeline up to the end of the last
    4411              :                          * complete record, so if we did this, we would later create an
    4412              :                          * overwrite contrecord in the wrong place, breaking everything.
    4413              :                          */
    4414            1 :                         if (!ArchiveRecoveryRequested &&

    4543              : /*

    4630              : /*
    4631              :  * I/O routines for pg_control
    4632              :  *
    4633              :  * *ControlFile is a buffer in shared memory that holds an image of the
    4634              :  * contents of pg_control.  WriteControlFile() initializes pg_control
    4635              :  * given a preloaded buffer, ReadControlFile() loads the buffer from
    4636              :  * the pg_control file (during postmaster or standalone-backend startup),
    4637              :  * and UpdateControlFile() rewrites pg_control after we modify xlog state.
    4638              :  * InitControlFile() fills the buffer with initial values.
    4639              :  *
    4640              :  * For simplicity, WriteControlFile() initializes the fields of pg_control
    4641              :  * that are related to checking backend/database compatibility, and
    4642              :  * ReadControlFile() verifies they are correct.  We could split out the
    4643              :  * I/O and compatibility-check functions, but there seems no need currently.
    4644              :  */
    4645              : 
    4646              : static void
    4647            1 : InitControlFile(uint64 sysidentifier)
    4648              : {
    4649              :         char            mock_auth_nonce[MOCK_AUTH_NONCE_LEN];
    4650              : 
    4651              :         /*
    4652              :          * Generate a random nonce. This is used for authentication requests that
    4653              :          * will fail because the user does not exist. The nonce is used to create
    4654              :          * a genuine-looking password challenge for the non-existent user, in lieu
    4655              :          * of an actual stored password.
    4656              :          */
    4657            1 :         if (!pg_strong_random(mock_auth_nonce, MOCK_AUTH_NONCE_LEN))
    4658            0 :                 ereport(PANIC,
    4659              :                                 (errcode(ERRCODE_INTERNAL_ERROR),
    4660              :                                  errmsg("could not generate secret authorization token")));
    4661              : 
    4662            1 :         memset(ControlFile, 0, sizeof(ControlFileData));
    4663              :         /* Initialize pg_control status fields */
    4664            1 :         ControlFile->system_identifier = sysidentifier;
    4665            1 :         memcpy(ControlFile->mock_authentication_nonce, mock_auth_nonce, MOCK_AUTH_NONCE_LEN);
    4666            1 :         ControlFile->state = DB_SHUTDOWNED;
    4667            1 :         ControlFile->unloggedLSN = FirstNormalUnloggedLSN;
    4668              : 
    4669              :         /* Set important parameter values for use when replaying WAL */
    4670            1 :         ControlFile->MaxConnections = MaxConnections;
    4671            1 :         ControlFile->max_worker_processes = max_worker_processes;
    4672            1 :         ControlFile->max_wal_senders = max_wal_senders;
    4673            1 :         ControlFile->max_prepared_xacts = max_prepared_xacts;
    4674            1 :         ControlFile->max_locks_per_xact = max_locks_per_xact;
    4675            1 :         ControlFile->wal_level = wal_level;
    4676            1 :         ControlFile->wal_log_hints = wal_log_hints;
    4677            1 :         ControlFile->track_commit_timestamp = track_commit_timestamp;
    4678            1 :         ControlFile->data_checksum_version = bootstrap_data_checksum_version;
    4679            1 : }

    4686              :

    4695              : 
    4696              :         /*

    4701              : 
    4702            1 :         ControlFile->maxAlign = MAXIMUM_ALIGNOF;
    4703            1 :         ControlFile->floatFormat = FLOATFORMAT_VALUE;
    4704              : 
    4705            1 :         ControlFile->blcksz = BLCKSZ;
    4706            1 :         ControlFile->relseg_size = RELSEG_SIZE;

    4709              : 
    4710            1 :         ControlFile->nameDataLen = NAMEDATALEN;

    5008              : /*
    5009              :  * Returns a fake LSN for unlogged relations.
    5010              :  *
    5011              :  * Each call generates an LSN that is greater than any previous value
    5012              :  * returned. The current counter value is saved and restored across clean
    5013              :  * shutdowns, but like unlogged relations, does not survive a crash. This can
    5014              :  * be used in lieu of real LSN values returned by XLogInsert, if you need an

    5025            0 :         SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->ulsn_lck);
    5026              : 
    5027            0 :         return nextUnloggedLSN;

    5029              : 
    5030              : /*
    5031              :  * Auto-tune the number of XLOG buffers.
    5032              :  *
    5033              :  * The preferred setting for wal_buffers is about 3% of shared_buffers, with
    5034              :  * a maximum of one XLOG segment (there is little reason to think that more
    5035              :  * is helpful, at least so long as we force an fsync when switching log files)
    5036              :  * and a minimum of 8 blocks (which was the default value prior to PostgreSQL
    5037              :  * 9.1, when auto-tuning was added).
    5038              :  *
    5039              :  * This should not be called until NBuffers has received its final value.
    5040              :  */
    5041              : static int
    5042            6 : XLOGChooseNumBuffers(void)
    5043              : {
    5044              :         int                     xbuffers;
    5045              : 
    5046            6 :         xbuffers = NBuffers / 32;
    5047            6 :         if (xbuffers > (wal_segment_size / XLOG_BLCKSZ))
    5048            0 :                 xbuffers = (wal_segment_size / XLOG_BLCKSZ);
    5049            6 :         if (xbuffers < 8)
    5050            0 :                 xbuffers = 8;
    5051            6 :         return xbuffers;
    5052              : }
    5053              : 
    5054              : /*
    5055              :  * GUC check_hook for wal_buffers
    5056              :  */
    5057              : bool
    5058           12 : check_wal_buffers(int *newval, void **extra, GucSource source)
    5059              : {
    5060              :         /*
    5061              :          * -1 indicates a request for auto-tune.
    5062              :          */
    5063           12 :         if (*newval == -1)
    5064              :         {
    5065              :                 /*
    5066              :                  * If we haven't yet changed the boot_val default of -1, just let it
    5067              :                  * be.  We'll fix it when XLOGShmemSize is called.
    5068              :                  */
    5069            6 :                 if (XLOGbuffers == -1)
    5070            6 :                         return true;
    5071              : 
    5072              :                 /* Otherwise, substitute the auto-tune value */
    5073            0 :                 *newval = XLOGChooseNumBuffers();
    5074              :         }
    5075              : 
    5076              :         /*
    5077              :          * We clamp manually-set values to at least 4 blocks.  Prior to PostgreSQL
    5078              :          * 9.1, a minimum of 4 was enforced by guc.c, but since that is no longer
    5079              :          * the case, we just silently treat such values as a request for the
    5080              :          * minimum.  (We could throw an error instead, but that doesn't seem very
    5081              :          * helpful.)
    5082              :          */
    5083            6 :         if (*newval < 4)
    5084            0 :                 *newval = 4;
    5085              : 
    5086            6 :         return true;
    5087              : }

    5089              : /*
    5090              :  * Read the control file, set respective GUCs.
    5091              :  *
    5092              :  * This is to be called during startup, including a crash recovery cycle,
    5093              :  * unless in bootstrap mode, where no control file yet exists.  As there's no
    5094              :  * usable shared memory yet (its sizing can depend on the contents of the
    5095              :  * control file!), first store the contents in local memory. XLOGShmemInit()
    5096              :  * will then copy it to shared memory later.
    5097              :  *

    5099              :  * reset case, there's a dangling pointer into old shared memory), or not.

    5101              : void
    5102            3 : LocalProcessControlFile(bool reset)
    5103              : {
    5104              :         Assert(reset || ControlFile == NULL);
    5105            3 :         ControlFile = palloc(sizeof(ControlFileData));
    5106            3 :         ReadControlFile();
    5107            3 : }

    5109              : /*
    5110              :  * Initialization of shared memory for XLOG

    5113           12 : XLOGShmemSize(void)
    5114              : {
    5115              :         Size            size;
    5116              : 
    5117              :         /*
    5118              :          * If the value of wal_buffers is -1, use the preferred auto-tune value.
    5119              :          * This isn't an amazingly clean place to do this, but we must wait till
    5120              :          * NBuffers has received its final value, and must do it before using the

    5123           12 :         if (XLOGbuffers == -1)
    5124              :         {
    5125              :                 char            buf[32];
    5126              : 
    5127            6 :                 snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "%d", XLOGChooseNumBuffers());
    5128            6 :                 SetConfigOption("wal_buffers", buf, PGC_POSTMASTER, PGC_S_OVERRIDE);
    5129              :         }
    5130              :         Assert(XLOGbuffers > 0);
    5131              : 
    5132              :         /* XLogCtl */

    5143              : 
    5144              :         /*

    5149              : 
    5150           12 :         return size;
    5151              : }

    5261              : /*
    5262              :  * This func must be called ONCE on system install.  It creates pg_control
    5263              :  * and the initial XLOG segment.
    5264              :  */
    5265              : void
    5266            1 : BootStrapXLOG(void)
    5267              : {
    5268              :         CheckPoint      checkPoint;
    5269              :         char       *buffer;
    5270              :         XLogPageHeader page;
    5271              :         XLogLongPageHeader longpage;
    5272              :         XLogRecord *record;
    5273              :         char       *recptr;
    5274              :         uint64          sysidentifier;
    5275              :         struct timeval tv;
    5276              :         pg_crc32c       crc;
    5277              : 
    5278              :         /* allow ordinary WAL segment creation, like StartupXLOG() would */
    5279            1 :         LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
    5280            1 :         XLogCtl->InstallXLogFileSegmentActive = true;
    5281            1 :         LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
    5282              : 
    5283              :         /*
    5284              :          * Select a hopefully-unique system identifier code for this installation.
    5285              :          * We use the result of gettimeofday(), including the fractional seconds
    5286              :          * field, as being about as unique as we can easily get.  (Think not to
    5287              :          * use random(), since it hasn't been seeded and there's no portable way
    5288              :          * to seed it other than the system clock value...)  The upper half of the
    5289              :          * uint64 value is just the tv_sec part, while the lower half contains the
    5290              :          * tv_usec part (which must fit in 20 bits), plus 12 bits from our current
    5291              :          * PID for a little extra uniqueness.  A person knowing this encoding can
    5292              :          * determine the initialization time of the installation, which could
    5293              :          * perhaps be useful sometimes.
    5294              :          */
    5295            1 :         gettimeofday(&tv, NULL);
    5296            1 :         sysidentifier = ((uint64) tv.tv_sec) << 32;
    5297            1 :         sysidentifier |= ((uint64) tv.tv_usec) << 12;
    5298            1 :         sysidentifier |= getpid() & 0xFFF;
    5299              : 
    5300              :         /* First timeline ID is always 1 */
    5301            1 :         ThisTimeLineID = 1;
    5302              : 
    5303              :         /* page buffer must be aligned suitably for O_DIRECT */
    5304            1 :         buffer = (char *) palloc(XLOG_BLCKSZ + XLOG_BLCKSZ);
    5305            1 :         page = (XLogPageHeader) TYPEALIGN(XLOG_BLCKSZ, buffer);
    5306            1 :         memset(page, 0, XLOG_BLCKSZ);
    5307              : 
    5308              :         /*
    5309              :          * Set up information for the initial checkpoint record
    5310              :          *
    5311              :          * The initial checkpoint record is written to the beginning of the WAL
    5312              :          * segment with logid=0 logseg=1. The very first WAL segment, 0/0, is not
    5313              :          * used, so that we can use 0/0 to mean "before any valid WAL segment".
    5314              :          */
    5315            1 :         checkPoint.redo = wal_segment_size + SizeOfXLogLongPHD;
    5316            1 :         checkPoint.ThisTimeLineID = ThisTimeLineID;
    5317            1 :         checkPoint.PrevTimeLineID = ThisTimeLineID;
    5318            1 :         checkPoint.fullPageWrites = fullPageWrites;
    5319              :         checkPoint.nextFullXid =
    5320            1 :                 FullTransactionIdFromEpochAndXid(0, FirstNormalTransactionId);
    5321            1 :         checkPoint.nextOid = FirstBootstrapObjectId;
    5322            1 :         checkPoint.nextMulti = FirstMultiXactId;
    5323            1 :         checkPoint.nextMultiOffset = 0;
    5324            1 :         checkPoint.oldestXid = FirstNormalTransactionId;
    5325            1 :         checkPoint.oldestXidDB = TemplateDbOid;
    5326            1 :         checkPoint.oldestMulti = FirstMultiXactId;
    5327            1 :         checkPoint.oldestMultiDB = TemplateDbOid;
    5328            1 :         checkPoint.oldestCommitTsXid = InvalidTransactionId;
    5329            1 :         checkPoint.newestCommitTsXid = InvalidTransactionId;
    5330            1 :         checkPoint.time = (pg_time_t) time(NULL);
    5331            1 :         checkPoint.oldestActiveXid = InvalidTransactionId;
    5332              : 
    5333            1 :         ShmemVariableCache->nextFullXid = checkPoint.nextFullXid;
    5334            1 :         ShmemVariableCache->nextOid = checkPoint.nextOid;
    5335            1 :         ShmemVariableCache->oidCount = 0;
    5336            1 :         MultiXactSetNextMXact(checkPoint.nextMulti, checkPoint.nextMultiOffset);
    5337            1 :         AdvanceOldestClogXid(checkPoint.oldestXid);
    5338            1 :         SetTransactionIdLimit(checkPoint.oldestXid, checkPoint.oldestXidDB);
    5339            1 :         SetMultiXactIdLimit(checkPoint.oldestMulti, checkPoint.oldestMultiDB, true);
    5340            1 :         SetCommitTsLimit(InvalidTransactionId, InvalidTransactionId);
    5341              : 
    5342              :         /* Set up the XLOG page header */
    5343            1 :         page->xlp_magic = XLOG_PAGE_MAGIC;
    5344            1 :         page->xlp_info = XLP_LONG_HEADER;
    5345            1 :         page->xlp_tli = ThisTimeLineID;
    5346            1 :         page->xlp_pageaddr = wal_segment_size;
    5347            1 :         longpage = (XLogLongPageHeader) page;
    5348            1 :         longpage->xlp_sysid = sysidentifier;
    5349            1 :         longpage->xlp_seg_size = wal_segment_size;
    5350            1 :         longpage->xlp_xlog_blcksz = XLOG_BLCKSZ;
    5351              : 
    5352              :         /* Insert the initial checkpoint record */

    6136              : /*
    6137              :  * When recovery_min_apply_delay is set, we wait long enough to make sure
    6138              :  * certain record types are applied at least that interval behind the master.
    6139              :  *
    6140              :  * Returns true if we waited.
    6141              :  *
    6142              :  * Note that the delay is calculated between the WAL record log time and
    6143              :  * the current time on standby. We would prefer to keep track of when this
    6144              :  * standby received each WAL record, which would allow a more consistent
    6145              :  * approach and one not affected by time synchronisation issues, but that
    6146              :  * is significantly more effort and complexity for little actual gain in
    6147              :  * usability.
    6148              :  */
    6149              : static bool
    6150            0 : recoveryApplyDelay(XLogReaderState *record)
    6151              : {

    6153              :         TimestampTz xtime;
    6154              :         TimestampTz delayUntil;
    6155              :         long            msecs;
    6156              : 
    6157              :         /* nothing to do if no delay configured */
    6158            0 :         if (recovery_min_apply_delay <= 0)
    6159            0 :                 return false;
    6160              : 
    6161              :         /* no delay is applied on a database not yet consistent */
    6162            0 :         if (!reachedConsistency)
    6163            0 :                 return false;
    6164              : 
    6165              :         /* nothing to do if crash recovery is requested */

    6167            0 :                 return false;
    6168              : 
    6169              :         /*
    6170              :          * Is it a COMMIT record?

    6174              :          * so there is already opportunity for issues caused by early conflicts on

    6176              :          */

    6178            0 :                 return false;
    6179              : 
    6180            0 :         xact_info = XLogRecGetInfo(record) & XLOG_XACT_OPMASK;
    6181              : 
    6182            0 :         if (xact_info != XLOG_XACT_COMMIT &&
    6183              :                 xact_info != XLOG_XACT_COMMIT_PREPARED)
    6184            0 :                 return false;
    6185              : 
    6186            0 :         if (!getRecordTimestamp(record, &xtime))
    6187            0 :                 return false;
    6188              : 
    6189            0 :         delayUntil = TimestampTzPlusMilliseconds(xtime, recovery_min_apply_delay);
    6190              : 
    6191              :         /*
    6192              :          * Exit without arming the latch if it's already past time to apply this

    6199              :         while (true)

    6217              : 
    6218              :                 /*

    6237              : /*

    6329              : /*
    6330              :  * Check to see if required parameters are set high enough on this server
    6331              :  * for various aspects of recovery operation.
    6332              :  *
    6333              :  * Note that all the parameters which this function tests need to be
    6334              :  * listed in Administrator's Overview section in high-availability.sgml.
    6335              :  * If you change them, don't forget to update the list.
    6336              :  */
    6337              : static void
    6338            1 : CheckRequiredParameterValues(void)
    6339              : {
    6340              :         /*
    6341              :          * For archive recovery, the WAL must be generated with at least 'replica'
    6342              :          * wal_level.
    6343              :          */
    6344            1 :         if (ArchiveRecoveryRequested && ControlFile->wal_level == WAL_LEVEL_MINIMAL)
    6345              :         {
    6346            0 :                 ereport(WARNING,
    6347              :                                 (errmsg("WAL was generated with wal_level=minimal, data may be missing"),
    6348              :                                  errhint("This happens if you temporarily set wal_level=minimal without taking a new base backup.")));
    6349              :         }
    6350              : 
    6351              :         /*
    6352              :          * For Hot Standby, the WAL must be generated with 'replica' mode, and we
    6353              :          * must have at least as many backend slots as the primary.
    6354              :          */
    6355            1 :         if (ArchiveRecoveryRequested && EnableHotStandby)
    6356              :         {
    6357            1 :                 if (ControlFile->wal_level < WAL_LEVEL_REPLICA)
    6358            0 :                         ereport(ERROR,
    6359              :                                         (errmsg("hot standby is not possible because wal_level was not set to \"replica\" or higher on the master server"),
    6360              :                                          errhint("Either set wal_level to \"replica\" on the master, or turn off hot_standby here.")));
    6361              : 
    6362              :                 /* We ignore autovacuum_max_workers when we make this test. */

    6364              :                                                                          MaxConnections,

    6366            1 :                 RecoveryRequiresIntParameter("max_worker_processes",
    6367              :                                                                          max_worker_processes,
    6368              :                                                                          ControlFile->max_worker_processes);
    6369            1 :                 RecoveryRequiresIntParameter("max_wal_senders",
    6370              :                                                                          max_wal_senders,
    6371              :                                                                          ControlFile->max_wal_senders);
    6372            1 :                 RecoveryRequiresIntParameter("max_prepared_transactions",

    6710              :                 /*
    6711              :                  * It's possible that archive recovery was requested, but we don't
    6712              :                  * know how far we need to replay the WAL before we reach consistency.

    6714              :                  * running server using an atomic filesystem snapshot, without calling
    6715              :                  * pg_start/stop_backup. Or if you just kill a running master server
    6716              :                  * and put it into archive recovery by creating a recovery signal
    6717              :                  * file.
    6718              :                  *
    6719              :                  * Our strategy in that case is to perform crash recovery first,
    6720              :                  * replaying all the WAL present in pg_wal, and only enter archive
    6721              :                  * recovery after that.
    6722              :                  *
    6723              :                  * But usually we already know how far we need to replay the WAL (up
    6724              :                  * to minRecoveryPoint, up to backupEndPoint, or until we see an

    6763              :         /*

    6767              :          * in place if the database had been cleanly shut down, but it seems

    6769              :          * first backend startup.  These files needs to be removed from all

    7303              :                                  * here.  We might therefore read a slightly stale value of

    7305              :                                  * worse than the last spinlock we did acquire).  Since a
    7306              :                                  * pause request is a pretty asynchronous thing anyway,

    7557              :         /*
    7558              :          * Kill WAL receiver, if it's still running, before we continue to write

    7564              : 
    7565              :         /*

    7568              :          * created during recovery, but BEFORE recovery is marked as having
    7569              :          * completed successfully. Otherwise we'd not retry if any of the post

    7571              :          */

    7573            1 :                 ResetUnloggedRelations(UNLOGGED_RELATION_INIT);
    7574              : 
    7575              :         /*

    7581              : 
    7582              :         /*

    7665              :         /*

    7687            1 :                 ThisTimeLineID = findNewestTimeLine(recoveryTargetTLI) + 1;

    7871              :                                 /*

    7904              :         /*
    7905              :          * Invalidate all sinval-managed caches before READ WRITE transactions
    7906              :          * begin.  The xl_heap_inplace WAL record doesn't store sufficient data
    7907              :          * for invalidations.  The commit record, if any, has the invalidations.
    7908              :          * However, the inplace update is permanent, whether or not we reach a
    7909              :          * commit record.  Fortunately, read-only transactions tolerate caches not
    7910              :          * reflecting the latest inplace updates.  Read-only transactions
    7911              :          * experience the notable inplace updates as follows:
    7912              :          *
    7913              :          * - relhasindex=true affects readers only after the CREATE INDEX
    7914              :          * transaction commit makes an index fully available to them.
    7915              :          *
    7916              :          * - datconnlimit=DATCONNLIMIT_INVALID_DB affects readers only at
    7917              :          * InitPostgres() time, and that read does not use a cache.
    7918              :          *
    7919              :          * - relfrozenxid, datfrozenxid, relminmxid, and datminmxid have no effect
    7920              :          * on readers.
    7921              :          *
    7922              :          * Hence, hot standby queries (all READ ONLY) function correctly without
    7923              :          * the missing invalidations.  This avoided changing the WAL format in
    7924              :          * back branches.
    7925              :          */

    7995              : 
    7996              :                 /*
    7997              :                  * We switched to a new timeline. Clean up segments on the old
    7998              :                  * timeline.
    7999              :                  *
    8000              :                  * If there are any higher-numbered segments on the old timeline,
    8001              :                  * remove them. They might contain valid WAL, but they might also be
    8002              :                  * pre-allocated files containing garbage. In any case, they are not

    8005            1 :                 RemoveNonParentXlogFiles(EndOfLog, ThisTimeLineID);
    8006              : 
    8007              :                 /*
    8008              :                  * If the switch happened in the middle of a segment, what to do with
    8009              :                  * the last, partial segment on the old timeline? If we don't archive
    8010              :                  * it, and the server that created the WAL never archives it either
    8011              :                  * (e.g. because it was hit by a meteor), it will never make it to the
    8012              :                  * archive. That's OK from our point of view, because the new segment
    8013              :                  * that we created with the new TLI contains all the WAL from the old
    8014              :                  * timeline up to the switch point. But if you later try to do PITR to
    8015              :                  * the "missing" WAL on the old timeline, recovery won't find it in
    8016              :                  * the archive. It's physically present in the new file with new TLI,
    8017              :                  * but recovery won't look there when it's recovering to the older
    8018              :                  * timeline. On the other hand, if we archive the partial segment, and
    8019              :                  * the original server on that timeline is still running and archives
    8020              :                  * the completed version of the same segment later, it will fail. (We
    8021              :                  * used to do that in 9.4 and below, and it caused such problems).
    8022              :                  *
    8023              :                  * As a compromise, we rename the last segment with the .partial
    8024              :                  * suffix, and archive it. Archive recovery will never try to read
    8025              :                  * .partial segments, so they will normally go unused. But in the odd
    8026              :                  * PITR case, the administrator can copy them manually to the pg_wal
    8027              :                  * directory (removing the suffix). They can be useful in debugging,
    8028              :                  * too.
    8029              :                  *
    8030              :                  * If a .done or .ready file already exists for the old timeline,
    8031              :                  * however, we had already determined that the segment is complete, so
    8032              :                  * we can let it be archived normally. (In particular, if it was
    8033              :                  * restored from the archive to begin with, it's expected to have a
    8034              :                  * .done file).
    8035              :                  */
    8036            1 :                 if (XLogSegmentOffset(EndOfLog, wal_segment_size) != 0 &&
    8037              :                         XLogArchivingActive())
    8038              :                 {
    8039              :                         char            origfname[MAXFNAMELEN];
    8040              :                         XLogSegNo       endLogSegNo;
    8041              : 
    8042            0 :                         XLByteToPrevSeg(EndOfLog, endLogSegNo, wal_segment_size);
    8043            0 :                         XLogFileName(origfname, EndOfLogTLI, endLogSegNo, wal_segment_size);
    8044              : 
    8045            0 :                         if (!XLogArchiveIsReadyOrDone(origfname))
    8046              :                         {
    8047              :                                 char            origpath[MAXPGPATH];
    8048              :                                 char            partialfname[MAXFNAMELEN];
    8049              :                                 char            partialpath[MAXPGPATH];
    8050              : 
    8051            0 :                                 XLogFilePath(origpath, EndOfLogTLI, endLogSegNo, wal_segment_size);
    8052            0 :                                 snprintf(partialfname, MAXFNAMELEN, "%s.partial", origfname);
    8053            0 :                                 snprintf(partialpath, MAXPGPATH, "%s.partial", origpath);
    8054              : 
    8055              :                                 /*
    8056              :                                  * Make sure there's no .done or .ready file for the .partial
    8057              :                                  * file.
    8058              :                                  */
    8059            0 :                                 XLogArchiveCleanup(partialfname);
    8060              : 
    8061            0 :                                 durable_rename(origpath, partialpath, ERROR);
    8062            0 :                                 XLogArchiveNotify(partialfname);
    8063              :                         }
    8064              :                 }

    8067              :         /*

    8072              : 
    8073              :         /*
    8074              :          * All done with end-of-recovery actions.
    8075              :          *
    8076              :          * Now allow backends to write WAL and update the control file status in
    8077              :          * consequence.  The boolean flag allowing backends to write WAL is
    8078              :          * updated while holding ControlFileLock to prevent other backends to look
    8079              :          * at an inconsistent state of the control file in shared memory.  There
    8080              :          * is still a small window during which backends can write WAL and the
    8081              :          * control file is still referring to a system not in DB_IN_PRODUCTION
    8082              :          * state while looking at the on-disk control file.
    8083              :          *
    8084              :          * Also, although the boolean flag to allow WAL is probably atomic in
    8085              :          * itself, we use the info_lck here to ensure that there are no race

    8093            4 :         SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);

    8096              : 
    8097            4 :         UpdateControlFile();
    8098            4 :         LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);

    8100              :         /*

    8127              : 
    8128              : /*
    8129              :  * Verify that, in non-test mode, ./pg_tblspc doesn't contain any real
    8130              :  * directories.
    8131              :  *
    8132              :  * Replay of database creation XLOG records for databases that were later
    8133              :  * dropped can create fake directories in pg_tblspc.  By the time consistency
    8134              :  * is reached these directories should have been removed; here we verify
    8135              :  * that this did indeed happen.  This is to be called at the point where
    8136              :  * consistent state is reached.
    8137              :  *
    8138              :  * allow_in_place_tablespaces turns the PANIC into a WARNING, which is
    8139              :  * useful for testing purposes, and also allows for an escape hatch in case
    8140              :  * things go south.
    8141              :  */
    8142              : static void
    8143            1 : CheckTablespaceDirectory(void)
    8144              : {
    8145              :         DIR        *dir;
    8146              :         struct dirent *de;
    8147              : 
    8148            1 :         dir = AllocateDir("pg_tblspc");
    8149            3 :         while ((de = ReadDir(dir, "pg_tblspc")) != NULL)
    8150              :         {
    8151              :                 char        path[MAXPGPATH + 10];
    8152              : #ifndef WIN32
    8153              :                 struct stat st;
    8154              : #endif
    8155              : 
    8156              :                 /* Skip entries of non-oid names */
    8157            2 :                 if (strspn(de->d_name, "0123456789") != strlen(de->d_name))
    8158            2 :                         continue;
    8159              : 
    8160            0 :                 snprintf(path, sizeof(path), "pg_tblspc/%s", de->d_name);
    8161              : 
    8162              : #ifndef WIN32
    8163            0 :                 if (lstat(path, &st) < 0)
    8164            0 :                         ereport(LOG,
    8165              :                                         (errcode_for_file_access(),
    8166              :                                          errmsg("could not stat file \"%s\": %m",
    8167              :                                                         path)));
    8168            0 :                 else if (!S_ISLNK(st.st_mode))
    8169              : #else           /* WIN32 */
    8170              :                 if (!pgwin32_is_junction(path))
    8171              : #endif
    8172            0 :                         ereport(allow_in_place_tablespaces ? WARNING : PANIC,
    8173              :                                         (errcode(ERRCODE_DATA_CORRUPTED),
    8174              :                                          errmsg("unexpected directory entry \"%s\" found in %s",
    8175              :                                                         de->d_name, "pg_tblspc/"),
    8176              :                                          errdetail("All directory entries in pg_tblspc/ should be symbolic links."),
    8177              :                                          errhint("Remove those directories, or set allow_in_place_tablespaces to ON transiently to let recovery complete.")));
    8178              :         }
    8179            1 : }
    8180              : 
    8181              : /*
    8182              :  * Checks if recovery has reached a consistent state. When consistency is
    8183              :  * reached and we have a valid starting standby snapshot, tell postmaster
    8184              :  * that it can start accepting read-only connections.
    8185              :  */
    8186              : static void
    8187            1 : CheckRecoveryConsistency(void)
    8188              : {
    8189              :         XLogRecPtr      lastReplayedEndRecPtr;
    8190              : 
    8191              :         /*
    8192              :          * During crash recovery, we don't reach a consistent state until we've
    8193              :          * replayed all the WAL.
    8194              :          */
    8195            1 :         if (XLogRecPtrIsInvalid(minRecoveryPoint))
    8196            0 :                 return;
    8197              : 
    8198              :         Assert(InArchiveRecovery);
    8199              : 
    8200              :         /*
    8201              :          * assume that we are called in the startup process, and hence don't need
    8202              :          * a lock to read lastReplayedEndRecPtr
    8203              :          */
    8204            1 :         lastReplayedEndRecPtr = XLogCtl->lastReplayedEndRecPtr;
    8205              : 
    8206              :         /*
    8207              :          * Have we reached the point where our base backup was completed?
    8208              :          */
    8209            1 :         if (!XLogRecPtrIsInvalid(ControlFile->backupEndPoint) &&
    8210            0 :                 ControlFile->backupEndPoint <= lastReplayedEndRecPtr)
    8211              :         {
    8212              :                 /*
    8213              :                  * We have reached the end of base backup, as indicated by pg_control.
    8214              :                  * The data on disk is now consistent. Reset backupStartPoint and
    8215              :                  * backupEndPoint, and update minRecoveryPoint to make sure we don't
    8216              :                  * allow starting up at an earlier point even if recovery is stopped
    8217              :                  * and restarted soon after this.
    8218              :                  */
    8219            0 :                 elog(DEBUG1, "end of backup reached");
    8220              : 
    8221            0 :                 LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
    8222              : 
    8223            0 :                 if (ControlFile->minRecoveryPoint < lastReplayedEndRecPtr)
    8224            0 :                         ControlFile->minRecoveryPoint = lastReplayedEndRecPtr;
    8225              : 
    8226            0 :                 ControlFile->backupStartPoint = InvalidXLogRecPtr;
    8227            0 :                 ControlFile->backupEndPoint = InvalidXLogRecPtr;
    8228            0 :                 ControlFile->backupEndRequired = false;
    8229            0 :                 UpdateControlFile();
    8230              : 
    8231            0 :                 LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
    8232              :         }
    8233              : 
    8234              :         /*
    8235              :          * Have we passed our safe starting point? Note that minRecoveryPoint is
    8236              :          * known to be incorrectly set if ControlFile->backupEndRequired, until
    8237              :          * the XLOG_BACKUP_END arrives to advise us of the correct
    8238              :          * minRecoveryPoint. All we know prior to that is that we're not
    8239              :          * consistent yet.
    8240              :          */
    8241            1 :         if (!reachedConsistency && !ControlFile->backupEndRequired &&
    8242            1 :                 minRecoveryPoint <= lastReplayedEndRecPtr &&
    8243            1 :                 XLogRecPtrIsInvalid(ControlFile->backupStartPoint))
    8244              :         {
    8245              :                 /*
    8246              :                  * Check to see if the XLOG sequence contained any unresolved
    8247              :                  * references to uninitialized pages.
    8248              :                  */
    8249            1 :                 XLogCheckInvalidPages();
    8250              : 
    8251              :                 /*
    8252              :                  * Check that pg_tblspc doesn't contain any real directories. Replay
    8253              :                  * of Database/CREATE_* records may have created ficticious tablespace
    8254              :                  * directories that should have been removed by the time consistency
    8255              :                  * was reached.
    8256              :                  */
    8257            1 :                 CheckTablespaceDirectory();
    8258              : 
    8259            1 :                 reachedConsistency = true;
    8260            1 :                 ereport(LOG,
    8261              :                                 (errmsg("consistent recovery state reached at %X/%X",
    8262              :                                                 (uint32) (lastReplayedEndRecPtr >> 32),
    8263              :                                                 (uint32) lastReplayedEndRecPtr)));
    8264              :         }
    8265              : 
    8266              :         /*
    8267              :          * Have we got a valid starting snapshot that will allow queries to be
    8268              :          * run? If so, we can tell postmaster that the database is consistent now,
    8269              :          * enabling connections.
    8270              :          */
    8271            1 :         if (standbyState == STANDBY_SNAPSHOT_READY &&
    8272            1 :                 !LocalHotStandbyActive &&
    8273            1 :                 reachedConsistency &&
    8274              :                 IsUnderPostmaster)
    8275              :         {
    8276            1 :                 SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
    8277            1 :                 XLogCtl->SharedHotStandbyActive = true;
    8278            1 :                 SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
    8279              : 
    8280            1 :                 LocalHotStandbyActive = true;
    8281              : 
    8282            1 :                 SendPostmasterSignal(PMSIGNAL_BEGIN_HOT_STANDBY);
    8283              :         }
    8284              : }
    8285              : 
    8286              : /*
    8287              :  * Is the system still in recovery?
    8288              :  *
    8289              :  * Unlike testing InRecovery, this works in any process that's connected to
    8290              :  * shared memory.
    8291              :  *
    8292              :  * As a side-effect, we initialize the local TimeLineID and RedoRecPtr
    8293              :  * variables the first time we see that recovery is finished.
    8294              :  */
    8295              : bool
    8296       214742 : RecoveryInProgress(void)

    8771              :                  (flags & CHECKPOINT_CAUSE_XLOG) ? " wal" : "",
    8772              :                  (flags & CHECKPOINT_CAUSE_TIME) ? " time" : "",
    8773              :                  (flags & CHECKPOINT_FLUSH_ALL) ? " flush-all" : "");

    8776              : /*
    8777              :  * Log end of a checkpoint.

    8797              :         /* Accumulate checkpoint timing summary data, in milliseconds. */

    8810              :

    8831              :                  CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_added,
    8832              :                  CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_removed,
    8833              :                  CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_recycled,
    8834              :                  write_msecs / 1000, (int) (write_msecs % 1000),
    8835              :                  sync_msecs / 1000, (int) (sync_msecs % 1000),
    8836              :                  total_msecs / 1000, (int) (total_msecs % 1000),
    8837              :                  CheckpointStats.ckpt_sync_rels,
    8838              :                  longest_msecs / 1000, (int) (longest_msecs % 1000),
    8839              :                  average_msecs / 1000, (int) (average_msecs % 1000),
    8840              :                  (int) (PrevCheckPointDistance / 1024.0),
    8841              :                  (int) (CheckPointDistanceEstimate / 1024.0));

    8843              : 
    8844              : /*
    8845              :  * Update the estimate of distance between checkpoints.
    8846              :  *
    8847              :  * The estimate is used to calculate the number of WAL segments to keep
    8848              :  * preallocated, see XLOGfileslop().
    8849              :  */
    8850              : static void
    8851           21 : UpdateCheckPointDistanceEstimate(uint64 nbytes)
    8852              : {
    8853              :         /*
    8854              :          * To estimate the number of segments consumed between checkpoints, keep a
    8855              :          * moving average of the amount of WAL generated in previous checkpoint
    8856              :          * cycles. However, if the load is bursty, with quiet periods and busy
    8857              :          * periods, we want to cater for the peak load. So instead of a plain
    8858              :          * moving average, let the average decline slowly if the previous cycle
    8859              :          * used less WAL than estimated, but bump it up immediately if it used
    8860              :          * more.
    8861              :          *
    8862              :          * When checkpoints are triggered by max_wal_size, this should converge to
    8863              :          * CheckpointSegments * wal_segment_size,
    8864              :          *
    8865              :          * Note: This doesn't pay any attention to what caused the checkpoint.
    8866              :          * Checkpoints triggered manually with CHECKPOINT command, or by e.g.
    8867              :          * starting a base backup, are counted the same as those created
    8868              :          * automatically. The slow-decline will largely mask them out, if they are
    8869              :          * not frequent. If they are frequent, it seems reasonable to count them
    8870              :          * in as any others; if you issue a manual checkpoint every 5 minutes and
    8871              :          * never let a timed checkpoint happen, it makes sense to base the
    8872              :          * preallocation on that 5 minute interval rather than whatever
    8873              :          * checkpoint_timeout is set to.
    8874              :          */
    8875           21 :         PrevCheckPointDistance = nbytes;
    8876           21 :         if (CheckPointDistanceEstimate < nbytes)
    8877            7 :                 CheckPointDistanceEstimate = nbytes;
    8878              :         else
    8879           14 :                 CheckPointDistanceEstimate =
    8880           14 :                         (0.90 * CheckPointDistanceEstimate + 0.10 * (double) nbytes);
    8881           21 : }
    8882              : 
    8883              : /*
    8884              :  * Perform a checkpoint --- either during shutdown, or on-the-fly
    8885              :  *
    8886              :  * flags is a bitwise OR of the following:
    8887              :  *      CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN: checkpoint is for database shutdown.
    8888              :  *      CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY: checkpoint is for end of WAL recovery.
    8889              :  *      CHECKPOINT_IMMEDIATE: finish the checkpoint ASAP,
    8890              :  *              ignoring checkpoint_completion_target parameter.
    8891              :  *      CHECKPOINT_FORCE: force a checkpoint even if no XLOG activity has occurred
    8892              :  *              since the last one (implied by CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN or
    8893              :  *              CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY).
    8894              :  *      CHECKPOINT_FLUSH_ALL: also flush buffers of unlogged tables.
    8895              :  *
    8896              :  * Note: flags contains other bits, of interest here only for logging purposes.
    8897              :  * In particular note that this routine is synchronous and does not pay
    8898              :  * attention to CHECKPOINT_WAIT.
    8899              :  *
    8900              :  * If !shutdown then we are writing an online checkpoint. This is a very special
    8901              :  * kind of operation and WAL record because the checkpoint action occurs over
    8902              :  * a period of time yet logically occurs at just a single LSN. The logical
    8903              :  * position of the WAL record (redo ptr) is the same or earlier than the
    8904              :  * physical position. When we replay WAL we locate the checkpoint via its
    8905              :  * physical position then read the redo ptr and actually start replay at the
    8906              :  * earlier logical position. Note that we don't write *anything* to WAL at
    8907              :  * the logical position, so that location could be any other kind of WAL record.
    8908              :  * All of this mechanism allows us to continue working while we checkpoint.
    8909              :  * As a result, timing of actions is critical here and be careful to note that
    8910              :  * this function will likely take minutes to execute on a busy system.
    8911              :  */

    8928              :         /*

    8934              :         else
    8935           16 :                 shutdown = false;
    8936              : 
    8937              :         /* sanity check */

    8939            0 :                 elog(ERROR, "can't create a checkpoint during recovery");
    8940              : 
    8941              :         /*
    8942              :          * Initialize InitXLogInsert working areas before entering the critical

    8948              :          */
    8949           21 :         InitXLogInsert();
    8950              : 
    8951              :         /*

    9155              :         /*

    9159              :          * performing those groups of actions.
    9160              :          *
    9161              :          * One example is end of transaction, so we must wait for any transactions
    9162              :          * that are currently in commit critical sections.  If an xact inserted
    9163              :          * its commit record into XLOG just before the REDO point, then a crash
    9164              :          * restart from the REDO point would not replay that record, which means
    9165              :          * that our flushing had better include the xact's update of pg_xact.  So
    9166              :          * we wait till he's out of his commit critical section before proceeding.

    9170              :          * fuzzy: it is possible that we will wait for xacts we didn't really need
    9171              :          * to wait for.  But the delay should be short and it seems better to make
    9172              :          * checkpoint take a bit longer than to hold off insertions longer than
    9173              :          * necessary. (In fact, the whole reason we have this issue is that xact.c
    9174              :          * does commit record XLOG insertion and clog update as two separate steps

    9176              :          * minimizing lock contention.)

    9369              : /*

    9421              : /*
    9422              :  * Write an OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD message.
    9423              :  *
    9424              :  * When on WAL replay we expect a continuation record at the start of a page
    9425              :  * that is not there, recovery ends and WAL writing resumes at that point.
    9426              :  * But it's wrong to resume writing new WAL back at the start of the record
    9427              :  * that was broken, because downstream consumers of that WAL (physical
    9428              :  * replicas) are not prepared to "rewind".  So the first action after
    9429              :  * finishing replay of all valid WAL must be to write a record of this type
    9430              :  * at the point where the contrecord was missing; to support xlogreader
    9431              :  * detecting the special case, XLP_FIRST_IS_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD is also added
    9432              :  * to the page header where the record occurs.  xlogreader has an ad-hoc
    9433              :  * mechanism to report metadata about the broken record, which is what we
    9434              :  * use here.
    9435              :  *
    9436              :  * At replay time, XLP_FIRST_IS_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD instructs xlogreader to
    9437              :  * skip the record it was reading, and pass back the LSN of the skipped
    9438              :  * record, so that its caller can verify (on "replay" of that record) that the
    9439              :  * XLOG_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD matches what was effectively overwritten.
    9440              :  */

    9442            0 : CreateOverwriteContrecordRecord(XLogRecPtr aborted_lsn)

    9446              : 
    9447              :         /* sanity check */

    9454            0 :         START_CRIT_SECTION();

    9456            0 :         XLogBeginInsert();

    9458              : 
    9459            0 :         recptr = XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD);
    9460              : 
    9461            0 :         XLogFlush(recptr);
    9462              : 
    9463            0 :         END_CRIT_SECTION();
    9464              : 
    9465            0 :         return recptr;
    9466              : }

    9785              :         /*

    9794              :         /*

    9796              :          * the oldest XMIN of any running transaction.  No future transaction will

    9823              : 
    9824            0 :         return true;

    9827              : /*
    9828              :  * Report availability of WAL for the given target LSN
    9829              :  *              (typically a slot's restart_lsn)
    9830              :  *
    9831              :  * Returns one of the following enum values:
    9832              :  *
    9833              :  * * WALAVAIL_RESERVED means targetLSN is available and it is in the range of
    9834              :  *   max_wal_size.
    9835              :  *
    9836              :  * * WALAVAIL_EXTENDED means it is still available by preserving extra
    9837              :  *   segments beyond max_wal_size. If max_slot_wal_keep_size is smaller
    9838              :  *   than max_wal_size, this state is not returned.
    9839              :  *
    9840              :  * * WALAVAIL_UNRESERVED means it is being lost and the next checkpoint will
    9841              :  *   remove reserved segments. The walsender using this slot may return to the
    9842              :  *   above.
    9843              :  *
    9844              :  * * WALAVAIL_REMOVED means it has been removed. A replication stream on
    9845              :  *   a slot with this LSN cannot continue after a restart.
    9846              :  *
    9847              :  * * WALAVAIL_INVALID_LSN means the slot hasn't been set to reserve WAL.
    9848              :  */

    9850            0 : GetWALAvailability(XLogRecPtr targetLSN)
    9851              : {

    9854              :         XLogSegNo       targetSeg;              /* segid of targetLSN */
    9855              :         XLogSegNo       oldestSeg;              /* actual oldest segid */
    9856              :         XLogSegNo       oldestSegMaxWalSize;    /* oldest segid kept by max_wal_size */
    9857              :         XLogSegNo       oldestSlotSeg;  /* oldest segid kept by slot */
    9858              :         uint64          keepSegs;
    9859              :         XLogRecPtr      slotsMinReqLSN;
    9860              : 
    9861              :         /*
    9862              :          * slot does not reserve WAL. Either deactivated, or has never been active

    9866              : 
    9867              :         /*
    9868              :          * Calculate the oldest segment currently reserved by all slots,

    9870              :          * oldestSlotSeg to the current segment.
    9871              :          */
    9872            0 :         currpos = GetXLogWriteRecPtr();
    9873            0 :         slotsMinReqLSN = XLogGetReplicationSlotMinimumLSN();

    9878              :          * Find the oldest extant segment file. We get 1 until checkpoint removes

    9883              : 
    9884              :         /* calculate oldest segment by max_wal_size */

    9891            0 :                 oldestSegMaxWalSize = 1;
    9892              : 
    9893              :         /* the segment we care about */
    9894            0 :         XLByteToSeg(targetLSN, targetSeg, wal_segment_size);

    9896              :         /*
    9897              :          * No point in returning reserved or extended status values if the

    9901              :         {
    9902              :                 /* show "reserved" when targetSeg is within max_wal_size */
    9903            0 :                 if (targetSeg >= oldestSegMaxWalSize)
    9904            0 :                         return WALAVAIL_RESERVED;
    9905              : 
    9906              :                 /* being retained by slots exceeding max_wal_size */
    9907            0 :                 return WALAVAIL_EXTENDED;
    9908              :         }
    9909              : 
    9910              :         /* WAL segments are no longer retained but haven't been removed yet */
    9911            0 :         if (targetSeg >= oldestSeg)
    9912            0 :                 return WALAVAIL_UNRESERVED;
    9913              : 
    9914              :         /* Definitely lost */
    9915            0 :         return WALAVAIL_REMOVED;
    9916              : }
    9917              : 
    9918              : 
    9919              : /*
    9920              :  * Retreat *logSegNo to the last segment that we need to retain because of
    9921              :  * either wal_keep_size or replication slots.
    9922              :  *
    9923              :  * This is calculated by subtracting wal_keep_size from the given xlog
    9924              :  * location, recptr and by making sure that that result is below the
    9925              :  * requirement of replication slots.  For the latter criterion we do consider
    9926              :  * the effects of max_slot_wal_keep_size: reserve at most that much space back
    9927              :  * from recptr.
    9928              :  *
    9929              :  * Note about replication slots: if this function calculates a value
    9930              :  * that's further ahead than what slots need reserved, then affected
    9931              :  * slots need to be invalidated and this function invoked again.

    9933              :  * invalidation is optionally done here, instead.
    9934              :  */

    9936           21 : KeepLogSeg(XLogRecPtr recptr, XLogRecPtr slotsMinReqLSN, XLogSegNo *logSegNo)
    9937              : {
    9938              :         XLogSegNo       currSegNo;
    9939              :         XLogSegNo       segno;
    9940              :         XLogRecPtr      keep;
    9941              : 
    9942           21 :         XLByteToSeg(recptr, currSegNo, wal_segment_size);
    9943           21 :         segno = currSegNo;
    9944              : 
    9945              :         /*
    9946              :          * Calculate how many segments are kept by slots first, adjusting for
    9947              :          * max_slot_wal_keep_size.
    9948              :          */
    9949           21 :         keep = slotsMinReqLSN;
    9950           21 :         if (keep != InvalidXLogRecPtr && keep < recptr)
    9951              :         {
    9952            0 :                 XLByteToSeg(keep, segno, wal_segment_size);
    9953              : 
    9954              :                 /* Cap by max_slot_wal_keep_size ... */
    9955            0 :                 if (max_slot_wal_keep_size_mb >= 0)
    9956              :                 {
    9957              :                         uint64          slot_keep_segs;
    9958              : 
    9959            0 :                         slot_keep_segs =
    9960            0 :                                 ConvertToXSegs(max_slot_wal_keep_size_mb, wal_segment_size);
    9961              : 
    9962            0 :                         if (currSegNo - segno > slot_keep_segs)
    9963            0 :                                 segno = currSegNo - slot_keep_segs;
    9964              :                 }
    9965              :         }
    9966              : 
    9967              :         /* but, keep at least wal_keep_size if that's set */
    9968           21 :         if (wal_keep_size_mb > 0)
    9969              :         {
    9970              :                 uint64          keep_segs;
    9971              : 
    9972            0 :                 keep_segs = ConvertToXSegs(wal_keep_size_mb, wal_segment_size);
    9973            0 :                 if (currSegNo - segno < keep_segs)
    9974              :                 {
    9975              :                         /* avoid underflow, don't go below 1 */
    9976            0 :                         if (currSegNo <= keep_segs)
    9977            0 :                                 segno = 1;
    9978              :                         else
    9979            0 :                                 segno = currSegNo - keep_segs;
    9980              :                 }
    9981              :         }
    9982              : 
    9983              :         /* don't delete WAL segments newer than the calculated segment */
    9984           21 :         if (segno < *logSegNo)
    9985            0 :                 *logSegNo = segno;

    9988              : /*
    9989              :  * Write a NEXTOID log record
    9990              :  */
    9991              : void
    9992            4 : XLogPutNextOid(Oid nextOid)
    9993              : {
    9994            4 :         XLogBeginInsert();
    9995            4 :         XLogRegisterData((char *) (&nextOid), sizeof(Oid));
    9996            4 :         (void) XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_NEXTOID);
    9997              : 
    9998              :         /*
    9999              :          * We need not flush the NEXTOID record immediately, because any of the
   10000              :          * just-allocated OIDs could only reach disk as part of a tuple insert or
   10001              :          * update that would have its own XLOG record that must follow the NEXTOID
   10002              :          * record.  Therefore, the standard buffer LSN interlock applied to those
   10003              :          * records will ensure no such OID reaches disk before the NEXTOID record
   10004              :          * does.
   10005              :          *
   10006              :          * Note, however, that the above statement only covers state "within" the
   10007              :          * database.  When we use a generated OID as a file or directory name, we
   10008              :          * are in a sense violating the basic WAL rule, because that filesystem
   10009              :          * change may reach disk before the NEXTOID WAL record does.  The impact
   10010              :          * of this is that if a database crash occurs immediately afterward, we
   10011              :          * might after restart re-generate the same OID and find that it conflicts
   10012              :          * with the leftover file or directory.  But since for safety's sake we
   10013              :          * always loop until finding a nonconflicting filename, this poses no real
   10014              :          * problem in practice. See pgsql-hackers discussion 27-Sep-2006.
   10015              :          */
   10016            4 : }
   10017              : 
   10018              : /*
   10019              :  * Write an XLOG SWITCH record.
   10020              :  *
   10021              :  * Here we just blindly issue an XLogInsert request for the record.
   10022              :  * All the magic happens inside XLogInsert.
   10023              :  *
   10024              :  * The return value is either the end+1 address of the switch record,
   10025              :  * or the end+1 address of the prior segment if we did not need to
   10026              :  * write a switch record because we are already at segment start.
   10027              :  */

   10033              :         /* XLOG SWITCH has no data */
   10034            0 :         XLogBeginInsert();
   10035              : 
   10036            0 :         if (mark_unimportant)
   10037            0 :                 XLogSetRecordFlags(XLOG_MARK_UNIMPORTANT);
   10038            0 :         RecPtr = XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_SWITCH);
   10039              : 
   10040            0 :         return RecPtr;
   10041              : }

   10067              : /*
   10068              :  * Check if any of the GUC parameters that are critical for hot standby

   10070              :  */
   10071              : static void
   10072            4 : XLogReportParameters(void)
   10073              : {
   10074            4 :         if (wal_level != ControlFile->wal_level ||
   10075            4 :                 wal_log_hints != ControlFile->wal_log_hints ||
   10076            4 :                 MaxConnections != ControlFile->MaxConnections ||
   10077            4 :                 max_worker_processes != ControlFile->max_worker_processes ||
   10078            4 :                 max_wal_senders != ControlFile->max_wal_senders ||
   10079            4 :                 max_prepared_xacts != ControlFile->max_prepared_xacts ||
   10080            4 :                 max_locks_per_xact != ControlFile->max_locks_per_xact ||
   10081            4 :                 track_commit_timestamp != ControlFile->track_commit_timestamp)
   10082              :         {
   10083              :                 /*
   10084              :                  * The change in number of backend slots doesn't need to be WAL-logged
   10085              :                  * if archiving is not enabled, as you can't start archive recovery
   10086              :                  * with wal_level=minimal anyway. We don't really care about the
   10087              :                  * values in pg_control either if wal_level=minimal, but seems better
   10088              :                  * to keep them up-to-date to avoid confusion.
   10089              :                  */
   10090            1 :                 if (wal_level != ControlFile->wal_level || XLogIsNeeded())
   10091              :                 {
   10092              :                         xl_parameter_change xlrec;
   10093              :                         XLogRecPtr      recptr;
   10094              : 
   10095            1 :                         xlrec.MaxConnections = MaxConnections;
   10096            1 :                         xlrec.max_worker_processes = max_worker_processes;
   10097            1 :                         xlrec.max_wal_senders = max_wal_senders;
   10098            1 :                         xlrec.max_prepared_xacts = max_prepared_xacts;
   10099            1 :                         xlrec.max_locks_per_xact = max_locks_per_xact;
   10100            1 :                         xlrec.wal_level = wal_level;
   10101            1 :                         xlrec.wal_log_hints = wal_log_hints;
   10102            1 :                         xlrec.track_commit_timestamp = track_commit_timestamp;
   10103              : 
   10104            1 :                         XLogBeginInsert();
   10105            1 :                         XLogRegisterData((char *) &xlrec, sizeof(xlrec));
   10106              : 
   10107            1 :                         recptr = XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_PARAMETER_CHANGE);
   10108            1 :                         XLogFlush(recptr);
   10109              :                 }
   10110              : 
   10111            1 :                 LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);

   10116            1 :                 ControlFile->max_prepared_xacts = max_prepared_xacts;
   10117            1 :                 ControlFile->max_locks_per_xact = max_locks_per_xact;
   10118            1 :                 ControlFile->wal_level = wal_level;
   10119            1 :                 ControlFile->wal_log_hints = wal_log_hints;
   10120            1 :                 ControlFile->track_commit_timestamp = track_commit_timestamp;

   10127              : /*
   10128              :  * Update full_page_writes in shared memory, and write an
   10129              :  * XLOG_FPW_CHANGE record if necessary.
   10130              :  *
   10131              :  * Note: this function assumes there is no other process running
   10132              :  * concurrently that could update it.
   10133              :  */
   10134              : void
   10135            6 : UpdateFullPageWrites(void)
   10136              : {
   10137            6 :         XLogCtlInsert *Insert = &XLogCtl->Insert;
   10138              :         bool            recoveryInProgress;
   10139              : 
   10140              :         /*
   10141              :          * Do nothing if full_page_writes has not been changed.
   10142              :          *
   10143              :          * It's safe to check the shared full_page_writes without the lock,
   10144              :          * because we assume that there is no concurrently running process which
   10145              :          * can update it.
   10146              :          */
   10147            6 :         if (fullPageWrites == Insert->fullPageWrites)
   10148            5 :                 return;
   10149              : 
   10150              :         /*
   10151              :          * Perform this outside critical section so that the WAL insert
   10152              :          * initialization done by RecoveryInProgress() doesn't trigger an
   10153              :          * assertion failure.
   10154              :          */
   10155            1 :         recoveryInProgress = RecoveryInProgress();

   10157            1 :         START_CRIT_SECTION();
   10158              : 
   10159              :         /*
   10160              :          * It's always safe to take full page images, even when not strictly
   10161              :          * required, but not the other round. So if we're setting full_page_writes
   10162              :          * to true, first set it true and then write the WAL record. If we're

   10166            1 :         if (fullPageWrites)
   10167              :         {
   10168            1 :                 WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();

   10170            1 :                 WALInsertLockRelease();
   10171              :         }
   10172              : 
   10173              :         /*
   10174              :          * Write an XLOG_FPW_CHANGE record. This allows us to keep track of
   10175              :          * full_page_writes during archive recovery, if required.
   10176              :          */

   10178              :         {
   10179            0 :                 XLogBeginInsert();
   10180            0 :                 XLogRegisterData((char *) (&fullPageWrites), sizeof(bool));
   10181              : 
   10182            0 :                 XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_FPW_CHANGE);
   10183              :         }
   10184              : 
   10185            1 :         if (!fullPageWrites)
   10186              :         {
   10187            0 :                 WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();

   10189            0 :                 WALInsertLockRelease();
   10190              :         }
   10191            1 :         END_CRIT_SECTION();
   10192              : }

   10194              : /*
   10195              :  * Check that it's OK to switch to new timeline during recovery.
