   10201            0 : checkTimeLineSwitch(XLogRecPtr lsn, TimeLineID newTLI, TimeLineID prevTLI)
   10202              : {
   10203              :         /* Check that the record agrees on what the current (old) timeline is */
   10204            0 :         if (prevTLI != ThisTimeLineID)
   10205            0 :                 ereport(PANIC,
   10206              :                                 (errmsg("unexpected previous timeline ID %u (current timeline ID %u) in checkpoint record",
   10207              :                                                 prevTLI, ThisTimeLineID)));
   10208              : 
   10209              :         /*
   10210              :          * The new timeline better be in the list of timelines we expect to see,
   10211              :          * according to the timeline history. It should also not decrease.
   10212              :          */
   10213            0 :         if (newTLI < ThisTimeLineID || !tliInHistory(newTLI, expectedTLEs))
   10214            0 :                 ereport(PANIC,
   10215              :                                 (errmsg("unexpected timeline ID %u (after %u) in checkpoint record",
   10216              :                                                 newTLI, ThisTimeLineID)));
   10217              : 
   10218              :         /*
   10219              :          * If we have not yet reached min recovery point, and we're about to
   10220              :          * switch to a timeline greater than the timeline of the min recovery

   10222              :          * possibly visit the min recovery point on the correct timeline anymore.
   10223              :          * This can happen if there is a newer timeline in the archive that
   10224              :          * branched before the timeline the min recovery point is on, and you
   10225              :          * attempt to do PITR to the new timeline.
   10226              :          */

   10229            0 :                 newTLI > minRecoveryPointTLI)

   10231              :                                 (errmsg("unexpected timeline ID %u in checkpoint record, before reaching minimum recovery point %X/%X on timeline %u",
   10232              :                                                 newTLI,
   10233              :                                                 (uint32) (minRecoveryPoint >> 32),
   10234              :                                                 (uint32) minRecoveryPoint,
   10235              :                                                 minRecoveryPointTLI)));
   10236              : 
   10237              :         /* Looks good */
   10238            0 : }

   10240              : /*
   10241              :  * XLOG resource manager's routines
   10242              :  *
   10243              :  * Definitions of info values are in include/catalog/pg_control.h, though
   10244              :  * not all record types are related to control file updates.
   10245              :  */
   10246              : void
   10247            0 : xlog_redo(XLogReaderState *record)
   10248              : {
   10249            0 :         uint8           info = XLogRecGetInfo(record) & ~XLR_INFO_MASK;
   10250            0 :         XLogRecPtr      lsn = record->EndRecPtr;
   10251              : 
   10252              :         /* in XLOG rmgr, backup blocks are only used by XLOG_FPI records */
   10253              :         Assert(info == XLOG_FPI || info == XLOG_FPI_FOR_HINT ||
   10254              :                    !XLogRecHasAnyBlockRefs(record));
   10255              : 
   10256            0 :         if (info == XLOG_NEXTOID)
   10257              :         {
   10258              :                 Oid                     nextOid;
   10259              : 
   10260              :                 /*
   10261              :                  * We used to try to take the maximum of ShmemVariableCache->nextOid
   10262              :                  * and the recorded nextOid, but that fails if the OID counter wraps
   10263              :                  * around.  Since no OID allocation should be happening during replay
   10264              :                  * anyway, better to just believe the record exactly.  We still take

   10686              : /*
   10687              :  * Return the (possible) sync flag used for opening a file, depending on the
   10688              :  * value of the GUC wal_sync_method.
   10689              :  */
   10690              : static int
   10691           15 : get_sync_bit(int method)
   10692              : {
   10693           15 :         int                     o_direct_flag = 0;
   10694              : 
   10695              :         /* If fsync is disabled, never open in sync mode */
   10696           15 :         if (!enableFsync)
   10697            4 :                 return 0;
   10698              : 
   10699              :         /*
   10700              :          * Optimize writes by bypassing kernel cache with O_DIRECT when using
   10701              :          * O_SYNC/O_FSYNC and O_DSYNC.  But only if archiving and streaming are
   10702              :          * disabled, otherwise the archive command or walsender process will read
   10703              :          * the WAL soon after writing it, which is guaranteed to cause a physical
   10704              :          * read if we bypassed the kernel cache. We also skip the
   10705              :          * posix_fadvise(POSIX_FADV_DONTNEED) call in XLogFileClose() for the same
   10706              :          * reason.
   10707              :          *
   10708              :          * Never use O_DIRECT in walreceiver process for similar reasons; the WAL
   10709              :          * written by walreceiver is normally read by the startup process soon
   10710              :          * after its written. Also, walreceiver performs unaligned writes, which
   10711              :          * don't work with O_DIRECT, so it is required for correctness too.
   10712              :          */
   10713           11 :         if (!XLogIsNeeded() && !AmWalReceiverProcess())
   10714            0 :                 o_direct_flag = PG_O_DIRECT;
   10715              : 
   10716           11 :         switch (method)
   10717              :         {
   10718              :                         /*
   10719              :                          * enum values for all sync options are defined even if they are
   10720              :                          * not supported on the current platform.  But if not, they are
   10721              :                          * not included in the enum option array, and therefore will never
   10722              :                          * be seen here.
   10723              :                          */
   10724           11 :                 case SYNC_METHOD_FSYNC:
   10725              :                 case SYNC_METHOD_FSYNC_WRITETHROUGH:
   10726              :                 case SYNC_METHOD_FDATASYNC:
   10727           11 :                         return 0;
   10728              : #ifdef OPEN_SYNC_FLAG
   10729            0 :                 case SYNC_METHOD_OPEN:
   10730            0 :                         return OPEN_SYNC_FLAG | o_direct_flag;
   10731              : #endif

   10837              : 
   10838              : /*
   10839              :  * do_pg_start_backup
   10840              :  *
   10841              :  * Utility function called at the start of an online backup. It creates the
   10842              :  * necessary starting checkpoint and constructs the backup label file.
   10843              :  *
   10844              :  * There are two kind of backups: exclusive and non-exclusive. An exclusive
   10845              :  * backup is started with pg_start_backup(), and there can be only one active
   10846              :  * at a time. The backup and tablespace map files of an exclusive backup are
   10847              :  * written to $PGDATA/backup_label and $PGDATA/tablespace_map, and they are
   10848              :  * removed by pg_stop_backup().
   10849              :  *
   10850              :  * A non-exclusive backup is used for the streaming base backups (see
   10851              :  * src/backend/replication/basebackup.c). The difference to exclusive backups
   10852              :  * is that the backup label and tablespace map files are not written to disk.
   10853              :  * Instead, their would-be contents are returned in *labelfile and *tblspcmapfile,
   10854              :  * and the caller is responsible for including them in the backup archive as
   10855              :  * 'backup_label' and 'tablespace_map'. There can be many non-exclusive backups
   10856              :  * active at the same time, and they don't conflict with an exclusive backup
   10857              :  * either.
   10858              :  *
   10859              :  * tablespaces is required only when this function is called while
   10860              :  * the streaming base backup requested by pg_basebackup is running.
   10861              :  * NULL should be specified otherwise.
   10862              :  *
   10863              :  * tblspcmapfile is required mainly for tar format in windows as native windows
   10864              :  * utilities are not able to create symlinks while extracting files from tar.
   10865              :  * However for consistency, the same is used for all platforms.
   10866              :  *
   10867              :  * needtblspcmapfile is true for the cases (exclusive backup and for
   10868              :  * non-exclusive backup only when tar format is used for taking backup)
   10869              :  * when backup needs to generate tablespace_map file, it is used to
   10870              :  * embed escape character before newline character in tablespace path.
   10871              :  *
   10872              :  * Returns the minimum WAL location that must be present to restore from this
   10873              :  * backup, and the corresponding timeline ID in *starttli_p.
   10874              :  *
   10875              :  * Every successfully started non-exclusive backup must be stopped by calling
   10876              :  * do_pg_stop_backup() or do_pg_abort_backup().
   10877              :  *
   10878              :  * It is the responsibility of the caller of this function to verify the
   10879              :  * permissions of the calling user!
   10880              :  */
   10881              : XLogRecPtr
   10882            0 : do_pg_start_backup(const char *backupidstr, bool fast, TimeLineID *starttli_p,

   10885              :                                    bool needtblspcmapfile)
   10886              : {
   10887            0 :         bool            exclusive = (labelfile == NULL);
   10888            0 :         bool            backup_started_in_recovery = false;

   10909              :

   10925              : 
   10926              :         /*
   10927              :          * Mark backup active in shared memory.  We must do full-page WAL writes
   10928              :          * during an on-line backup even if not doing so at other times, because
   10929              :          * it's quite possible for the backup dump to obtain a "torn" (partially
   10930              :          * written) copy of a database page if it reads the page concurrently with
   10931              :          * our write to the same page.  This can be fixed as long as the first
   10932              :          * write to the page in the WAL sequence is a full-page write. Hence, we
   10933              :          * turn on forcePageWrites and then force a CHECKPOINT, to ensure there
   10934              :          * are no dirty pages in shared memory that might get dumped while the
   10935              :          * backup is in progress without having a corresponding WAL record.  (Once
   10936              :          * the backup is complete, we need not force full-page writes anymore,
   10937              :          * since we expect that any pages not modified during the backup interval
   10938              :          * must have been correctly captured by the backup.)
   10939              :          *
   10940              :          * Note that forcePageWrites has no effect during an online backup from
   10941              :          * the standby.
   10942              :          *
   10943              :          * We must hold all the insertion locks to change the value of
   10944              :          * forcePageWrites, to ensure adequate interlocking against

   10978              : 
   10979              :                 /*
   10980              :                  * Force an XLOG file switch before the checkpoint, to ensure that the
   10981              :                  * WAL segment the checkpoint is written to doesn't contain pages with
   10982              :                  * old timeline IDs.  That would otherwise happen if you called
   10983              :                  * pg_start_backup() right after restoring from a PITR archive: the
   10984              :                  * first WAL segment containing the startup checkpoint has pages in
   10985              :                  * the beginning with the old timeline ID.  That can cause trouble at
   10986              :                  * recovery: we won't have a history file covering the old timeline if
   10987              :                  * pg_wal directory was not included in the base backup and the WAL
   10988              :                  * archive was cleared too before starting the backup.
   10989              :                  *
   10990              :                  * This also ensures that we have emitted a WAL page header that has
   10991              :                  * XLP_BKP_REMOVABLE off before we emit the checkpoint record.
   10992              :                  * Therefore, if a WAL archiver (such as pglesslog) is trying to
   10993              :                  * compress out removable backup blocks, it won't remove any that
   10994              :                  * occur after this point.
   10995              :                  *
   10996              :                  * During recovery, we skip forcing XLOG file switch, which means that
   10997              :                  * the backup taken during recovery is not available for the special
   10998              :                  * recovery case described above.
   10999              :                  */

   11001            0 :                         RequestXLogSwitch(false);

   11003              :                 do
   11004              :                 {

   11006              : 
   11007              :                         /*
   11008              :                          * Force a CHECKPOINT.  Aside from being necessary to prevent torn
   11009              :                          * page problems, this guarantees that two successive backup runs

   11011              :                          * history file names, even if nothing happened in between.
   11012              :                          *
   11013              :                          * During recovery, establish a restartpoint if possible. We use
   11014              :                          * the last restartpoint as the backup starting checkpoint. This
   11015              :                          * means that two successive backup runs can have same checkpoint
   11016              :                          * positions.
   11017              :                          *
   11018              :                          * Since the fact that we are executing do_pg_start_backup()

   11625              : 
   11626              :         /*
   11627              :          * During recovery, we don't write an end-of-backup record. We assume that
   11628              :          * pg_control was backed up last and its minimum recovery point can be
   11629              :          * available as the backup end location. Since we don't have an
   11630              :          * end-of-backup record, we use the pg_control value to check whether
   11631              :          * we've reached the end of backup when starting recovery from this
   11632              :          * backup. We have no way of checking if pg_control wasn't backed up last
   11633              :          * however.
   11634              :          *
   11635              :          * We don't force a switch to new WAL file but it is still possible to
   11636              :          * wait for all the required files to be archived if waitforarchive is
   11637              :          * true. This is okay if we use the backup to start a standby and fetch
   11638              :          * the missing WAL using streaming replication. But in the case of an
   11639              :          * archive recovery, a user should set waitforarchive to true and wait for
   11640              :          * them to be archived to ensure that all the required files are
   11641              :          * available.
   11642              :          *
   11643              :          * We return the current minimum recovery point as the backup end
   11644              :          * location. Note that it can be greater than the exact backup end
   11645              :          * location if the minimum recovery point is updated after the backup of
   11646              :          * pg_control. This is harmless for current uses.
   11647              :          *
   11648              :          * XXX currently a backup history file is for informational and debug
   11649              :          * purposes only. It's not essential for an online backup. Furthermore,
   11650              :          * even if it's created, it will not be archived during recovery because
   11651              :          * an archiver is not invoked. So it doesn't seem worthwhile to write a
   11652              :          * backup history file during recovery.
   11653              :          */

   11655              :         {
   11656              :                 XLogRecPtr      recptr;
   11657              : 
   11658              :                 /*
   11659              :                  * Check to see if all WAL replayed during online backup contain
   11660              :                  * full-page writes.
   11661              :                  */
   11662            0 :                 SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
   11663            0 :                 recptr = XLogCtl->lastFpwDisableRecPtr;
   11664            0 :                 SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
   11665              : 
   11666            0 :                 if (startpoint <= recptr)
   11667            0 :                         ereport(ERROR,
   11668              :                                         (errcode(ERRCODE_OBJECT_NOT_IN_PREREQUISITE_STATE),

   11746              :         /*

   11751              :          * polling and copying files from pg_wal with script. We have no knowledge
   11752              :          * of those mechanisms, so it's up to the user to ensure that he gets all
   11753              :          * the required WAL.

   11756              :          * history file have been archived, and assume that the alphabetic sorting

   11758              :          * archived as well.
   11759              :          *
   11760              :          * We wait forever, since archive_command is supposed to work and we
   11761              :          * assume the admin wanted his backup to work completely. If you don't
   11762              :          * wish to wait, then either waitforarchive should be passed in as false,
   11763              :          * or you can set statement_timeout.  Also, some notices are issued to
   11764              :          * clue in anyone who might be doing this interactively.

   11825              : /*
   11826              :  * do_pg_abort_backup: abort a running backup
   11827              :  *
   11828              :  * This does just the most basic steps of do_pg_stop_backup(), by taking the
   11829              :  * system out of backup mode, thus making it a lot more safe to call from
   11830              :  * an error handler.
   11831              :  *
   11832              :  * The caller can pass 'arg' as 'true' or 'false' to control whether a warning
   11833              :  * is emitted.
   11834              :  *
   11835              :  * NB: This is only for aborting a non-exclusive backup that doesn't write
   11836              :  * backup_label. A backup started with pg_start_backup() needs to be finished
   11837              :  * with pg_stop_backup().
   11838              :  *
   11839              :  * NB: This gets used as a before_shmem_exit handler, hence the odd-looking
   11840              :  * signature.
   11841              :  */
   11842              : void
   11843            0 : do_pg_abort_backup(int code, Datum arg)
   11844              : {
   11845            0 :         bool            emit_warning = DatumGetBool(arg);
   11846              : 
   11847              :         /*
   11848              :          * Quick exit if session is not keeping around a non-exclusive backup
   11849              :          * already started.
   11850              :          */
   11851            0 :         if (sessionBackupState != SESSION_BACKUP_NON_EXCLUSIVE)
   11852            0 :                 return;
   11853              : 
   11854            0 :         WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();
   11855              :         Assert(XLogCtl->Insert.nonExclusiveBackups > 0);
   11856            0 :         XLogCtl->Insert.nonExclusiveBackups--;
   11857              : 
   11858            0 :         if (XLogCtl->Insert.exclusiveBackupState == EXCLUSIVE_BACKUP_NONE &&
   11859            0 :                 XLogCtl->Insert.nonExclusiveBackups == 0)
   11860              :         {
   11861            0 :                 XLogCtl->Insert.forcePageWrites = false;
   11862              :         }
   11863              : 
   11864            0 :         sessionBackupState = SESSION_BACKUP_NONE;
   11865            0 :         WALInsertLockRelease();
   11866              : 
   11867            0 :         if (emit_warning)
   11868            0 :                 ereport(WARNING,
   11869              :                                 (errmsg("aborting backup due to backend exiting before pg_stop_backup was called")));
   11870              : }
   11871              : 
   11872              : /*

   11880              : 
   11881            0 :         if (already_done)
   11882            0 :                 return;

   11950              : /*

   11965              :  */

   12080              : /*
   12081              :  * read_tablespace_map: check to see if a tablespace_map file is present
   12082              :  *
   12083              :  * If we see a tablespace_map file during recovery, we assume that we are

   12092            0 : read_tablespace_map(List **tablespaces)

   12103              : 
   12104              :         /*
   12105              :          * See if tablespace_map file is present

   12196              : /*

   12264              : /*
   12265              :  * Read the XLOG page containing RecPtr into readBuf (if not read already).

   12408              :         /*

   12413              :          * continuation records, if a record is split across two pages such that
   12414              :          * we would need to read the two pages from different sources across two
   12415              :          * WAL segments.

   12418              :          * already been recycled on the primary. The second page, however, is not
   12419              :          * present in pg_wal, and we should stream it from the primary. There is a
   12420              :          * WAL segment present in pg_wal, with garbage contents, however. We would
   12421              :          * read the first page from the local WAL segment, but when reading the
   12422              :          * second page, we would read the bogus, recycled, WAL segment. If we
   12423              :          * didn't catch that case here, we would never recover, because
   12424              :          * ReadRecord() would retry reading the whole record from the beginning.
   12425              :          *

   12427              :          * happens in the case of a recycled WAL segment. Other kinds of errors or
   12428              :          * corruption still has the same problem. But this at least fixes the
   12429              :          * common case, which can happen as part of normal operation.
   12430              :          *
   12431              :          * Validating the page header is cheap enough that doing it twice

   12474              : /*
   12475              :  * Open the WAL segment containing WAL location 'RecPtr'.
   12476              :  *

   12478              :  * streamed the record, or it can already be present in pg_wal. Checking
   12479              :  * pg_wal is mainly for crash recovery, but it will be polled in standby mode
   12480              :  * too, in case someone copies a new segment directly to pg_wal. That is not
   12481              :  * documented or recommended, though.
   12482              :  *

   12484              :  * prepare to read WAL starting from RedoStartLSN after this.
   12485              :  *
   12486              :  * 'RecPtr' might not point to the beginning of the record we're interested
   12487              :  * in, it might also point to the page or segment header. In that case,
   12488              :  * 'tliRecPtr' is the position of the WAL record we're interested in. It is
   12489              :  * used to decide which timeline to stream the requested WAL from.
   12490              :  *
   12491              :  * If the record is not immediately available, the function returns false
   12492              :  * if we're not in standby mode. In standby mode, waits for it to become
   12493              :  * available.
   12494              :  *
   12495              :  * When the requested record becomes available, the function opens the file
   12496              :  * containing it (if not open already), and returns true. When end of standby
   12497              :  * mode is triggered by the user, and there is no more WAL available, returns

   12501            5 : WaitForWALToBecomeAvailable(XLogRecPtr RecPtr, bool randAccess,

   12503              : {
   12504              :         static TimestampTz last_fail_time = 0;
   12505              :         TimestampTz now;
   12506            5 :         bool            streaming_reply_sent = false;
   12507              : 
   12508              :         /*-------
   12509              :          * Standby mode is implemented by a state machine:
   12510              :          *
   12511              :          * 1. Read from either archive or pg_wal (XLOG_FROM_ARCHIVE), or just
   12512              :          *        pg_wal (XLOG_FROM_PG_WAL)
   12513              :          * 2. Check trigger file
   12514              :          * 3. Read from primary server via walreceiver (XLOG_FROM_STREAM)
   12515              :          * 4. Rescan timelines
   12516              :          * 5. Sleep wal_retrieve_retry_interval milliseconds, and loop back to 1.
   12517              :          *
   12518              :          * Failure to read from the current source advances the state machine to
   12519              :          * the next state.
   12520              :          *
   12521              :          * 'currentSource' indicates the current state. There are no currentSource
   12522              :          * values for "check trigger", "rescan timelines", and "sleep" states,
   12523              :          * those actions are taken when reading from the previous source fails, as
   12524              :          * part of advancing to the next state.
   12525              :          *
   12526              :          * If standby mode is turned off while reading WAL from stream, we move
   12527              :          * to XLOG_FROM_ARCHIVE and reset lastSourceFailed, to force fetching
   12528              :          * the files (which would be required at end of recovery, e.g., timeline
   12529              :          * history file) from archive or pg_wal. We don't need to kill WAL receiver
   12530              :          * here because it's already stopped when standby mode is turned off at
   12531              :          * the end of recovery.
   12532              :          *-------
   12533              :          */

   12535            3 :                 currentSource = XLOG_FROM_PG_WAL;

   12537            1 :                          (!StandbyMode && currentSource == XLOG_FROM_STREAM))
   12538              :         {
   12539            1 :                 lastSourceFailed = false;
   12540            1 :                 currentSource = XLOG_FROM_ARCHIVE;
   12541              :         }
   12542              : 
   12543              :         for (;;)
   12544            0 :         {

   12546            5 :                 bool            startWalReceiver = false;
   12547              : 
   12548              :                 /*

   12551              :                  * happened outside this function, e.g when a CRC check fails on a
   12552              :                  * record, or within this loop.

   12554            5 :                 if (lastSourceFailed)
   12555              :                 {
   12556            0 :                         switch (currentSource)
   12557              :                         {
   12558            0 :                                 case XLOG_FROM_ARCHIVE:
   12559              :                                 case XLOG_FROM_PG_WAL:
   12560              : 
   12561              :                                         /*

   12564              :                                          * trigger file, we still finish replaying as much as we

   12566              :                                          */
   12567            0 :                                         if (StandbyMode && CheckForStandbyTrigger())
   12568              :                                         {
   12569            0 :                                                 XLogShutdownWalRcv();
   12570            0 :                                                 return false;
   12571              :                                         }
   12572              : 
   12573              :                                         /*
   12574              :                                          * Not in standby mode, and we've now tried the archive
   12575              :                                          * and pg_wal.
   12576              :                                          */
   12577            0 :                                         if (!StandbyMode)
   12578            0 :                                                 return false;
   12579              : 
   12580              :                                         /*
   12581              :                                          * Move to XLOG_FROM_STREAM state, and set to start a
   12582              :                                          * walreceiver if necessary.
   12583              :                                          */
   12584            0 :                                         currentSource = XLOG_FROM_STREAM;
   12585            0 :                                         startWalReceiver = true;
   12586            0 :                                         break;
   12587              : 
   12588            0 :                                 case XLOG_FROM_STREAM:
   12589              : 
   12590              :                                         /*
   12591              :                                          * Failure while streaming. Most likely, we got here
   12592              :                                          * because streaming replication was terminated, or
   12593              :                                          * promotion was triggered. But we also get here if we
   12594              :                                          * find an invalid record in the WAL streamed from master,
   12595              :                                          * in which case something is seriously wrong. There's
   12596              :                                          * little chance that the problem will just go away, but
   12597              :                                          * PANIC is not good for availability either, especially
   12598              :                                          * in hot standby mode. So, we treat that the same as
   12599              :                                          * disconnection, and retry from archive/pg_wal again. The
   12600              :                                          * WAL in the archive should be identical to what was
   12601              :                                          * streamed, so it's unlikely that it helps, but one can
   12602              :                                          * hope...
   12603              :                                          */
   12604              : 
   12605              :                                         /*
   12606              :                                          * We should be able to move to XLOG_FROM_STREAM only in
   12607              :                                          * standby mode.
   12608              :                                          */
   12609              :                                         Assert(StandbyMode);
   12610              : 
   12611              :                                         /*
   12612              :                                          * Before we leave XLOG_FROM_STREAM state, make sure that
   12613              :                                          * walreceiver is not active, so that it won't overwrite
   12614              :                                          * WAL that we restore from archive.
   12615              :                                          * If walreceiver is actively streaming (or attempting to
   12616              :                                          * connect), we must shut it down. However, if it's
   12617              :                                          * already in WAITING state (e.g., due to timeline
   12618              :                                          * divergence), we only need to reset the install flag to
   12619              :                                          * allow archive restoration.
   12620              :                                          */

   12622            0 :                                                 XLogShutdownWalRcv();

   12624              :                                         {
   12625            0 :                                                 ResetInstallXLogFileSegmentActive();
   12626              :                                         }
   12627              : 
   12628              :                                         /*
   12629              :                                          * Before we sleep, re-scan for possible new timelines if
   12630              :                                          * we were requested to recover to the latest timeline.
   12631              :                                          */

   12989              : /*
   12990              :  * Determine what log level should be used to report a corrupt WAL record

   12992              :  *
   12993              :  * 'emode' is the error mode that would be used to report a file-not-found
   12994              :  * or legitimate end-of-WAL situation.   Generally, we use it as-is, but if
   12995              :  * we're retrying the exact same record that we've tried previously, only
   12996              :  * complain the first time to keep the noise down.  However, we only do when
   12997              :  * reading from pg_wal, because we don't expect any invalid records in archive
   12998              :  * or in records streamed from master. Files in the archive should be complete,
   12999              :  * and we should never hit the end of WAL because we stop and wait for more WAL
   13000              :  * to arrive before replaying it.
   13001              :  *
   13002              :  * NOTE: This function remembers the RecPtr value it was last called with,
   13003              :  * to suppress repeated messages about the same record. Only call this when
   13004              :  * you are about to ereport(), or you might cause a later message to be
   13005              :  * erroneously suppressed.
   13006              :  */

   13008            1 : emode_for_corrupt_record(int emode, XLogRecPtr RecPtr)

   13015            0 :                         emode = DEBUG1;
   13016              :                 else
   13017            1 :                         lastComplaint = RecPtr;
   13018              :         }
   13019            1 :         return emode;
   13020              : }
